beats.title: Table of Contents of the Agni Purāṇa
beats.summary: This chapter is the table of contents (anukramaṇikā) of the Agni Purāṇa, listing the subjects of each chapter in the text. It begins with the initial question about the Purāṇa and then enumerates topics including the ten avatars of Viṣṇu (Matsya, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Kṛṣṇa, Buddha, Kalki), creation, worship procedures, temple construction, iconography, and various rituals.

beats.title: Invocation and the Quest for the Essence
beats.summary: The first chapter of the Agni Purana begins with a salutation to various deities, then narrates a dialogue where sages ask Suta for the essence of knowledge. Suta recounts how he and others approached Vyasa, who in turn relayed teachings from Vasishtha, who learned from Agni. The chapter establishes the two-fold Brahman (para and apara), with Vishnu as the supreme essence, and outlines the scope of the Purana covering creation, dissolution, dynasties, and Manvantaras.

beats.title: Matsya Avatar: The Fish Incarnation
beats.summary: Agni narrates the Matsya (fish) avatar of Vishnu to sage Vashishtha. During a cosmic dissolution, Manu Vaivasvata finds a small fish that grows rapidly, revealing itself as Vishnu. The fish warns of an impending flood and instructs Manu to build a boat, take seeds and the seven sages, and tie it to its horn. After the flood, Vishnu as Matsya kills the demon Hayagriva and restores the Vedas.

beats.title: Kurma Avatar and the Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Kurma (tortoise) avatar of Vishnu. It narrates the churning of the ocean of milk by the devas and asuras, with Mount Mandara as the churning rod and Vasuki as the rope. Vishnu takes the form of a tortoise to support the mountain. Various treasures emerge, including poison (halahala) swallowed by Shiva, the goddess Varuni, the Parijata tree, Kaustubha gem, celestial cows, apsaras, and Lakshmi. Dhanvantari appears with the amrita. Vishnu assumes the female form Mohini to distribute the amrita to the devas, deceiving the asuras. Rahu, an asura, tries to drink the amrita but is beheaded by Vishnu; his head becomes immortal and causes eclipses. Shiva, enchanted by Mohini, chases her, and his spilled semen creates sacred sites. The devas defeat the asuras and attain heaven.

beats.title: Avatars of Vishnu: Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, and Parashurama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the avatars of Vishnu: Varaha (boar), Narasimha (man-lion), Vamana (dwarf), and Parashurama (Rama with axe). It narrates the stories of Hiranyaksha's defeat, Hiranyakashipu's killing, Vamana's three steps, and Parashurama's destruction of the Kshatriyas.

beats.title: Shri Ramavatara Varnanam
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Rama, an avatar of Vishnu, from his birth to his marriage to Sita, including his training under Vishvamitra, slaying of demons, and breaking of Shiva's bow.

beats.title: Rama's Exile and Dasharatha's Death
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Rama's avatar. King Dasharatha prepares to crown Rama as prince, but Kaikeyi, instigated by Manthara, demands Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation. Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita go to the forest. Dasharatha dies of grief. Bharata refuses the throne and goes to bring Rama back, but Rama insists on fulfilling his father's promise. Bharata returns with Rama's sandals and rules from Nandigrama.

beats.title: Rama's Exile: Dandaka Forest and Abduction of Sita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana narrates the story of Rama's exile, focusing on events in the Dandaka forest: meeting sages, encounter with Surpanakha, killing of Khara and Dushana, abduction of Sita by Ravana with Maricha's help, and Rama's grief.

beats.title: Shri Ramavatara Kathana
beats.summary: Narada narrates the story of Rama's avatar: Rama goes to Pampa lake, meets Hanuman and Sugriva, makes friends with Sugriva, kills Vali, installs Sugriva as king of Kishkindha, spends the rainy season on Malyavat mountain, sends Lakshmana to remind Sugriva of his promise, then sends monkeys to search for Sita. Sampati tells them Sita is in Lanka.

beats.title: Hanuman's Journey to Lanka and Meeting with Sita
beats.summary: Hanuman leaps across the ocean to Lanka, finds Sita in Ashoka grove, delivers Rama's ring, battles demons, sets Lanka on fire, and returns to report to Rama and Sugriva.

beats.title: The War and Coronation of Rama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the war between Rama and Ravana, including the battle with Kumbhakarna, the death of Ravana, Rama's coronation, and his rule over Ayodhya.

beats.title: The Reign and Departure of Rama
beats.summary: Narada describes the aftermath of Rama's victory, including the visit of sages, the lineage of Ravana, the fates of Ravana's brothers and son, the rule of Rama, and his eventual departure to heaven.

beats.title: Harivamsha: Genealogy and Exploits of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana narrates the Harivamsha, the genealogy of Hari (Vishnu/Krishna). It traces the lineage from Brahma to the Yadava dynasty, focusing on the birth and early life of Krishna and Balarama, their exploits in Vrindavan, and later events including the story of Pradyumna and Aniruddha, the battle with Bana, and the reconciliation of Vishnu and Shiva.

beats.title: The Origin of the Kurus and Pandavas
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of the Kauravas and Pandavas, starting from Brahma, through the lunar dynasty, and culminating in the birth of Dhritarashtra, Pandu, and their sons. It describes how Vishnu incarnated to relieve the earth's burden by instigating the Kurukshetra war through the Pandavas.

beats.title: Description of the Kuru-Pandava War
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Kurukshetra war, starting with the armies' arrival, Bhishma's appointment as commander, the battle's progression over days, key duels, and the eventual fall of major warriors. It includes Krishna's teaching to Arjuna about the eternal soul and the importance of fulfilling one's duty.

beats.title: The Pandavas' Final Journey and the End of the Yadava Clan
beats.summary: After the Mahabharata war, Yudhishthira rules. Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti retire to the forest. Vidura dies in a forest fire. Vishnu, through the Pandavas, reduces the earth's burden, and by the pretext of a sage's curse, destroys the Yadava clan with an iron club. Krishna abandons his body at Prabhasa and is worshipped in Indra's and Brahma's realms. Balarama, as Ananta, goes to the underworld and heaven. The ocean floods Dvarka. Arjuna returns to Hastinapura, reports to Yudhishthira, and laments the loss of Krishna's weapons. Yudhishthira installs Parikshit on the throne and, with Draupadi and his brothers, sets out on the great journey, realizing the world's impermanence. Draupadi, Sahadeva, Nakula, Arjuna, and Bhima fall one by one. Yudhishthira alone reaches heaven, sees Duryodhana and others, and rejoices in Vasudeva.

beats.title: Buddha and Kalki Avataras
beats.summary: Agni describes the avataras of Buddha and Kalki. Buddha, as an avatar of Vishnu, deludes the demons to abandon Vedic dharma, leading to the rise of heretical sects. At the end of Kali Yuga, Kalki will appear to destroy the mlecchas and restore dharma.

beats.title: Creation of the Universe
beats.summary: Agni describes the process of creation from the unmanifest, through the evolution of mahat, ahamkara, and the five elements, to the formation of the cosmic egg and the birth of Brahma. The chapter also lists the mind-born sons of Brahma and the creation of the Vedas, celestial phenomena, and beings.

beats.title: Chapter 18
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineage of Svayambhuva Manu, including Priyavrata, Uttanapada, Dhruva, and their descendants. It details the birth of Prithu from Vena, the churning of the earth, and the genealogy of the Prachetasas, Daksha, and various deities and sages.

beats.title: The Lineage of Kashyapa
beats.summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana describes the lineage of Kashyapa, detailing the births of various beings including the Adityas, Daityas, Danavas, Gandharvas, Yakshas, Rakshasas, and others. It covers the progeny of Kashyapa's wives such as Aditi, Diti, Danu, and others, and includes the story of Indra's destruction of Diti's fetus, leading to the birth of the Maruts.

beats.title: The Nine Creations and Genealogy of Sages
beats.summary: Agni describes the nine types of creation (sarga) according to the Puranic cosmology: three prākṛta (primary) creations and six vaikṛta (secondary) creations, including the kaumāra (Brahma's mind-born) creation. He then lists the progeny of the sages and their wives, tracing the lineage from Dakṣa's daughters.

beats.title: Chapter 21
beats.summary: Nārada describes the general worship procedures for Viṣṇu, Śiva, and Sūrya, including mantras, deities to be invoked, and the order of offerings.

beats.title: Ritual Bathing Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for ritual bathing (snāna), including the use of clay associated with Narasimha, the distinction between mental and physical bathing, and the protective mantras to be recited.

beats.title: Pūjāvidhi (Worship Procedure) and Navavyūha Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the detailed procedure for worship (pūjā) of Viṣṇu, including purification, nyāsa, meditation, and offering of various items. It also outlines the worship of the nine Vyūhas (emanations) of Viṣṇu.

beats.title: Instructions on Kuṇḍa Construction and Fire Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions for constructing a fire altar (kuṇḍa) and performing fire rituals (homa) for the worship of Viṣṇu. It covers measurements, shapes, and procedures for the kuṇḍa, the preparation of offerings, the initiation of disciples, and the meditative visualization of cosmic creation and dissolution.

beats.title: Vāsudevādimantranirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Vāsudevādimantranirūpaṇa', provides a detailed exposition on the mantras and worship of Vāsudeva and related deities. It describes the construction of mantras using seed syllables (bījas), the arrangement of limbs (aṅgas) and sub-limbs (upāṅgas), and the placement of various cosmic principles (vyūhas) on the body and hands during meditation and ritual. The chapter also lists the twelve forms of Viṣṇu and outlines the benefits of worshipping the universal form (viśvarūpa) and other deities like Garuḍa and Indra.

beats.title: Description of Mudrās
beats.summary: This chapter describes the characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of mudrās (hand gestures) used in worship. It begins with the añjali mudrā and explains various hand positions for different deities, including Viśvarūpa, Garuḍa, Indra, Viśvaksena, and Varāha. The mudrās are categorized as common (sādharaṇā) and uncommon (asādharaṇā), with specific instructions for finger placements and movements to achieve siddhi (perfection).

beats.title: Dīkṣāvidhikathana: The Procedure of Initiation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana details the ritual of initiation (dīkṣā), including the construction of a mandala, purification of elements, and the process of liberating the disciple's soul from material bonds through mantras and offerings.

beats.title: Abhiṣeka Vidhāna
beats.summary: Nārada describes the ritual of abhiṣeka (consecration) for a disciple (putraka) or sādhaka, involving placement of pots, worship of Viṣṇu, and chanting, leading to success, healing, and attainment of kingdom, son, or wife.

beats.title: Sarvatobhadra Mandala Construction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes in detail the construction and layout of the Sarvatobhadra mandala, a sacred geometric diagram used in ritual worship. It provides precise measurements, divisions, and placement of lotus petals, petals, and other elements, along with instructions for drawing the mandala and the associated deity worship, particularly of Vishnu in various forms.

beats.title: Mandala Vidhi: The Procedure of the Mandala
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the construction and worship of a mandala (sacred diagram) for ritual purposes. It details the placement of deities, elements, and cosmic principles in various petals and enclosures, along with color specifications and mantra practices.

beats.title: Mārjanavidhāna: The Purification Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the mārjanavidhāna, a ritual purification and protection procedure. It includes invocations to various avatars of Vishnu (Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Trivikrama, Rama, etc.) and uses mantras to ward off diseases, poisons, evil spirits, and planetary afflictions. The text emphasizes the power of Vishnu's names and forms to destroy all obstacles and grant well-being.

beats.title: Enumeration of Samskaras and Yajnas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana enumerates the various samskaras (sacraments) and yajnas (sacrifices) that a wise person should perform, from conception to liberation. It lists 48 samskaras including garbhadhana, pumsavana, simantonnayana, jatakarma, namakarana, annaprashana, chudakarana, upanayana, and others. It also describes seven pakayajnas, seven haviryajnas, and seven somasamsthas (soma sacrifices), culminating in the ashvamedha. The chapter emphasizes that performing these rites leads to becoming a god (suro bhavet).

beats.title: Pavitrārohaṇa Vidhāna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of Pavitrārohaṇa (the raising of sacred threads) for the worship of Viṣṇu, including the preparation of pavitraka (sacred thread), the construction of a maṇḍala, the process of bhūtaśuddhi (purification of elements), mental worship (mānasa pūjā), and the installation of deities and guardians. It details the mantras, measurements, and procedures for the annual festival.

beats.title: Homa and Worship Procedures
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual procedures for homa (fire sacrifice) and related worship, including the construction of a mandala, installation of deities, purification rites, and the homa process itself. It details the use of mantras, offerings, and the roles of various divine entities.

beats.title: Ritual of Pavitrādhivāsana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of pavitrādhivāsana (consecration of sacred threads) and related procedures, including offerings, meditation on fire, and placement of various items in different directions using specific mantras.

beats.title: Pavitrāropaṇavidhāna: The Ritual of Offering Sacred Threads
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of pavitrāropaṇa (offering of sacred threads) to deities, including bathing, purification, mantra recitation, and offerings. It details the procedure for consecrating pavitraka (sacred thread) and vanamālā (garland) to Viṣṇu and other deities, with prayers for purification and forgiveness of ritual errors.

beats.title: Sarvadevapavitrārohaṇa Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of 'Sarvadevapavitrārohaṇa' (the installation of sacred threads for all deities). It provides mantras and procedures for offering pavitra (sacred thread) to various deities including Vishnu, Shiva, Surya, and others, along with the associated benefits.

beats.title: The Fruits of Building Temples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the immense spiritual benefits of building and maintaining temples, especially for Vishnu (Krishna). It details how constructing a temple, even with minimal resources, purifies one's lineage, grants heaven or liberation, and protects from hell. Yama instructs his messengers to avoid those involved in temple construction and worship. The chapter also notes that the Hayashirsha (Hayaśīrṣa) taught the procedure for consecration.

beats.title: Bhūparigrahavidhāna: The Procedure for Land Acquisition and Temple Installation
beats.summary: Hayagrīva describes the installation of deities, listing various Pañcarātra tantras, the qualifications of a guru, the proper orientation and placement of temples in a city, and the ritual of bhūparigraha (land acquisition) including offerings to spirits and measurement units.

beats.title: Arghya Dana Vidhana: The Ritual of Offering Water Libation to Vastupurusha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of offering arghya (water libation) to the Vastupurusha and various deities during the construction of a building. It details the placement of offerings at specific positions in the 64-pad mandala, the materials to be used, and the procedure for consecrating the site, including digging a pit, placing a pot with Vishnu, and performing a homa.

beats.title: Ritual for Foundation Laying (Śilāvinyāsa)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual procedure for laying the foundation of a building, specifically the placement of bricks and stones (śilāvinyāsa). It details the construction of a pavilion (maṇḍapa), the preparation of bricks, the installation of copper pots (kumbhas) with various substances, the invocation of deities, and the performance of homa (fire sacrifice). The chapter also includes mantras for consecrating the site and invoking blessings for prosperity and protection.

beats.title: Temple Construction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions on the construction of temples (prāsāda), including measurements, proportions, and placement of architectural elements such as pillars, doors, and spires. It also describes the layout of the temple complex, the installation of deities, and the auspicious rituals associated with building a temple.

beats.title: Establishment of Deities in Temples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides instructions for establishing deities in temples, focusing on the placement of various forms of Vishnu and other gods in different directions, the types of materials for making images, and the rituals for selecting and preparing stone for carving the deity. It includes mantras and procedures for propitiating spirits and ensuring auspiciousness.

beats.title: Instructions for Making Images of Vasudeva and Other Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions for creating and measuring the proportions of images of Vasudeva (Krishna) and other deities, including specific measurements for various body parts, guidelines for the placement of lines (sutras), and the process of carving the image from stone.

beats.title: Piṇḍikālakṣaṇakathanaṃ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the characteristics and measurements for constructing the pedestal (piṇḍikā) of an idol, along with the proportions for the idol itself, particularly for the goddess Lakṣmī. It provides detailed instructions on dimensions, divisions, and ornamentation.

beats.title: Description of Śālagrāma and Mūrti Characteristics
beats.summary: This chapter describes the characteristics of various Śālagrāma stones and their associated forms of Viṣṇu, including Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Nārāyaṇa, Nṛsiṃha, Varāha, Kūrma, Hayagrīva, Vaikuṇṭha, Matsya, Śrīdhara, Vāmana, Trivikrama, and Ananta. Each form is identified by specific features such as color, number of cakras, lines, and other marks on the stone.

beats.title: Śālagrāma Worship and Iconography
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Śālagrāma stones and other sacred objects, detailing the types of worship (kāmyā, akāmyā, ubhayātmikā), the arrangement of maṇḍalas, placement of deities, and the use of mantras and mudrās. It also lists the characteristics of various forms of Viṣṇu based on cakra marks.

beats.title: Caturviṃśatimūrtistotra: The Hymn of Twenty-Four Forms of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents the 'Caturviṃśatimūrtistotra' (hymn of twenty-four forms), where Lord Vishnu is praised in his twenty-four iconic forms, each holding specific combinations of the four attributes: conch (śaṅkha), discus (cakra), mace (gadā), and lotus (padma). The hymn enumerates the names and attributes of these forms, which are used in worship and meditation.

beats.title: Iconography of Avatars and Divine Forms
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the iconographic features of the ten avatars of Vishnu (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, Kalki) and the nine forms of Vasudeva (Vasudeva, Sankarshana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Narayana, Brahma, Vishnu, Narasimha, Varaha, etc.), detailing their attributes, postures, and associated deities.

beats.title: Iconography of Goddesses and Associated Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the iconographic characteristics of various goddesses, including Caṇḍī, Durgā, Gaurī, Lakṣmī, Sarasvatī, and others, as well as associated deities like Vināyaka, Skanda, and Rudracarcikā. It details their number of arms, weapons, mounts, and postures, and provides instructions for their placement and worship.

beats.title: Iconography of Surya and Other Deities
beats.summary: This chapter describes the iconography of Surya (the Sun god) and other deities, including planetary deities, Dikpalas (guardians of directions), and various celestial beings. It details their attributes, mounts, and associated colors.

beats.title: Iconography of Goddesses and Bhairava
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the iconography of various deities, including the eight groups of Yoginis, Bhairava, Virabhadra, Gauri, Lalita, and Chandika, detailing their attributes, weapons, mounts, and forms. It also provides instructions for worship and the construction of images.

beats.title: Characteristics of the Liṅga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of the Śiva liṅga, including its proportions, measurements, and the construction of the piṇḍikā (pedestal). It provides detailed instructions for carving the liṅga from stone, specifying dimensions based on divisions of the total height, and outlines the process for creating the base and the liṅga's shape.

beats.title: Linga Measurements and Types
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions on the measurements, proportions, and types of Shiva lingas, including materials (salt, ghee, cloth, clay, wood, stone, pearl, metal, etc.), classifications (cala, madhyama, jyeshtha), and methods for carving and installing them. It also describes the proportions of the linga's parts (brahma, vishnu, shiva) and the auspiciousness of various shapes and sizes.

beats.title: The Characteristics of the Pedestal
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the measurements and construction of the pedestal (piṇḍikā) for a deity's image, including proportions relative to the image, the mekhalā (girdle), the khāta (excavation), the praṇāla (water channel), and the īśa (the deity's seat).

beats.title: Worship of the Directional Guardians
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of worshiping the directional guardians (Dikpalas) during the consecration of a temple or image. It details the construction of the altar, placement of pots, invocation of deities like Indra, Agni, Yama, Nirriti, Varuna, Vayu, Soma, Ishana, Brahma, and Ananta, along with their mantras and offerings.

beats.title: Kumbhādhivāsa Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of consecrating pots (kumbhādhivāsa) for a ceremony. It details the preparation of the ground, placement of various pots with specific materials like grains, herbs, gems, and water from sacred sources, and the invocation of deities such as Vishnu, Lakshmi, and directional guardians. The procedure includes mantras, offerings, and the arrangement of items for worship.

beats.title: The Ritual of Consecration (Snānādividhi)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the detailed ritual for the consecration (snānādividhi) of an image of Vishnu, including the construction of a kuṇḍa, homa, procession, binding of kautuka, installation, anointing, bathing with various substances, offering of garments, ornaments, and concluding with hymns.

beats.title: Adhivāsana: Invoking the Presence of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of adhivāsana (invoking the presence of the deity) for installing an image of Viṣṇu. It details the meditative absorption of the cosmos into the self, the emanation of the vyūha forms (Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha) and Brahmā, the creation of the elements and sense organs, the assignment of seed mantras to body parts (nyāsa), the placement of the twelve forms of Viṣṇu (Keśava, Nārāyaṇa, etc.) on the body, the construction of a twelve-spoked cakra and lotus for worship, and the final homa (fire sacrifice) with specific mantras and offerings.

beats.title: Consecration of Vāsudeva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the detailed procedure for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of an image of Vāsudeva (Viṣṇu), including the preparation of the pedestal (piṇḍikā), the placement of gems (ratnanyāsa), purification rituals, homa (fire sacrifice), installation of the image, and the invocation of the deity's presence. It also covers the worship of attendant deities, offerings, and the final dedication.

beats.title: Rituals for Temple Door and Flag Installation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the rituals for establishing a temple door (dvārapratiṣṭhā) and hoisting a flag (dhvajārohaṇa). It details the placement of pots, offerings, homa, and the symbolic correspondence between the temple structure and the cosmic body of Viṣṇu. The chapter also explains the benefits of these rites, including attaining Viṣṇu's abode.

beats.title: Lakṣmīpratiṣṭhāvidhiḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for establishing (pratiṣṭhā) the goddess Lakṣmī and other deities. It details the procedures including placement on a bhadrapīṭha, anointing, bathing with pañcagavya, offering madhuratraya, abhiṣeka with various mantras from different directions, installation with śrīsūkta, homa with lotus or karavīra flowers, and final consecration. The chapter emphasizes the use of specific mantras and the importance of invoking the presence of the deity.

beats.title: Installation of Sudarśana Cakra and Other Deities
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the installation (pratiṣṭhā) of Sudarśana cakra and other divine weapons, along with the installation of various forms of Viṣṇu, including Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Vaikuṇṭha, Hayagrīva, Aniruddha, Matsya, etc. It also details the consecration of books (pustaka pratiṣṭhā), including the rituals for writing, installing, and worshiping manuscripts of the Purāṇas.

beats.title: Consecration of Water Bodies
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of wells, ponds, and tanks. It details the construction of images of Varuṇa, the placement of water pots, the performance of homa, and the benefits of donating water bodies. The chapter emphasizes the spiritual merit of providing water and equates it to performing thousands of horse sacrifices.

beats.title: Construction and Consecration of Assembly Halls
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides instructions for constructing and consecrating assembly halls (sabhā) and other buildings. It covers site selection, architectural proportions, directional considerations, and rituals for establishing the building, including mantras for invoking deities in the bricks.

beats.title: The General Consecration Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for the general consecration (sādhāraṇapratiṣṭhā) of deities, including the installation of images, fire offerings (homa), and the construction of temples, gardens, and rest houses. It details the mantras, materials, and rituals to be performed, emphasizing the worship of Viṣṇu and the benefits of such acts.

beats.title: Jīrṇoddhāravidhāna (Renovation Procedure)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for renovating (jīrṇoddhāra) a temple or idol. It covers the treatment of worn-out images, the rituals for dismantling and re-establishing, and the merit of restoring wells, ponds, and tanks.

beats.title: Procedure for the Festival of a Deity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for conducting a festival (utsava) for a deity, including the timing, preparation of sprouts (ankura), offerings, procession (yatra), and bathing rituals. It emphasizes that installation without a festival is fruitless.

beats.title: Snānavidhāna (Ritual Bath Procedure)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the detailed procedure for the ritual bath (snāna) and festival (utsava) of a deity, focusing on the arrangement of pots (kumbhas) in a mandala, the placement of various substances, and the accompanying rites such as meditation, worship, and fire sacrifice.

beats.title: Consecration of Trees and Gardens
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for consecrating trees and gardens (vṛkṣārāmapratiṣṭhā). It details the procedures for anointing, adorning, piercing ears, applying collyrium, offering bali, performing homa, and bathing the trees with Vedic mantras. The chapter also mentions the rewards of such consecration, including the destruction of sins and attainment of perfection.

beats.title: Gaṇeśapūjāvidhiḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual worship of Ganesha (Vinayaka) for removing obstacles and granting all desires. It includes mantras, nyasa, and offerings, as well as a list of names and attributes of Ganesha.

beats.title: Ritual Baths and Twilight Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various types of ritual baths (snāna) and the sandhyā (twilight) worship, including mantras, mudras, and meditations on deities. It details procedures for purification, tarpana (libations), and the significance of different tīrthas (sacred waters).

beats.title: Surya Puja Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual worship of the Sun god (Surya), including the use of mantras, mudras, nyasa, and offerings. It details the procedure for Surya puja, starting with meditation, then offering arghya, performing nyasa on the body, drawing a yantra, invoking the deity, and making offerings. The chapter also includes the worship of planetary deities and concludes with the benefits of such worship.

beats.title: Śivapūjākathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides a detailed manual for the ritual worship of Shiva (Śivapūjā). It covers purification, preparation of the worshipper, meditation on the five elements, installation of the deity, offering of various items, and concluding rites. The text emphasizes inner purification and the unity of the worshipper with Shiva.

beats.title: The Establishment and Worship of the Fire Altar
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions for the establishment and worship of the sacred fire (Agni) in a ritual context. It covers the construction of the fire altar (kuṇḍa), the purification and installation of fire, the invocation of deities, and the performance of homa (fire offerings) with various materials. The text emphasizes the use of mantras, mudras, and meditative visualizations to transform the physical fire into a divine presence, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga framework of internalizing external rituals.

beats.title: Canda Puja Kathana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of Canda, a fierce form of Shiva. It details the procedure for offering arghya, performing homa, and the specific mantras and mudras for Canda's puja, including the visualization of his form and the disposal of nirmalya.

beats.title: Kapilādipūjāvidhanaṃ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Kapilādipūjāvidhanaṃ', provides detailed instructions for the worship of the cow (Kapila) and associated rituals, including offerings, fire sacrifices, and the consecration of the kitchen and utensils. It emphasizes purity, mantra recitation, and the integration of bodily pranas with the elements.

beats.title: Pavitrārohaṇa Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of pavitrārohaṇa (consecration of sacred threads) for deities, detailing the materials, measurements, mantras, and procedures for installation and worship, with emphasis on Śiva.

beats.title: Pavitrārohaṇavidhiḥ: The Procedure for Offering the Sacred Thread
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Pavitrārohaṇavidhiḥ' (The Procedure for Offering the Sacred Thread), provides a detailed ritualistic guide for the pavitrārohaṇa ceremony. It describes the steps to be performed by a practitioner (mantrī) after morning purification, including the preparation of pavitra (sacred threads), worship of deities, homa (fire offerings), and the final offering to Śiva. The chapter emphasizes the importance of proper ritual conduct and the role of the guru.

beats.title: The Ritual of Offering Damanaka to Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of offering damanaka (a plant) to Lord Shiva, including its origin story, the procedure for plucking and consecrating the plant, and the benefits of performing this rite.

beats.title: The Ritual of Initiation (Dīkṣāvidhāna)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of initiation (dīkṣā) for both worldly enjoyment (bhoga) and liberation (mokṣa). It details the types of disciples based on their spiritual maturity, the two main forms of initiation (nirādhārā and sādhārā), and the elaborate procedure involving purification, mantra nyāsa, homa (fire offerings), and the final act of transferring divine power to the disciple. The chapter emphasizes the role of the guru as Śiva and the importance of proper ritual conduct.

beats.title: Saṃskāradīkṣākathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Saṃskāradīkṣākathana' (Description of the Initiation Rite), details the ritual procedure for initiation (dīkṣā) into the worship of Śiva. It includes instructions for invoking Śiva into fire, purifying the disciple, performing homa (fire sacrifice), and bestowing the sacred thread and other symbols of the Śaiva tradition. The chapter emphasizes the transformation of the disciple into a 'son of Rudra' through a series of symbolic acts corresponding to life-cycle rites (garbhādhāna, puṃsavana, sīmantonnayana, janana). It also outlines the duties and observances for the initiated, such as not criticizing the deity or scriptures, and performing daily worship of Śiva, fire, and the guru.

beats.title: Nirvāṇadīkṣāvidhi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of Nirvāṇa Dīkṣā (initiation for liberation), including the procedures for mantra illumination, binding of the disciple's subtle body, offerings, and the final consecration. It details the use of specific mantras, mudras, and homa (fire offerings) to purify and empower the disciple.

beats.title: Nirvāṇadīkṣā: Purification of Nivṛttikalā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of nirvāṇadīkṣā (liberation initiation), focusing on the purification of the nivṛttikalā (the aspect of cessation). It details the procedures for the guru, including morning rites, construction of a maṇḍala, worship of Śiva, homa (fire offerings), and the initiation of the disciple. The text lists numerous names of Rudra and other deities, explains the symbolism of mantras and bodily elements, and outlines the process of transferring the disciple's consciousness to Śiva, cutting the bonds of māyā, and bestowing liberation.

beats.title: The Ritual of Pratiṣṭhā: Purification of the Kalā and Liberation of the Paśu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of pratiṣṭhā (consecration) involving the purification of the kalā (vital energy) and the pāśa (bondage). It details the process of uniting the pure and impure tattvas, the use of mantras and mudras, the invocation of deities like Viṣṇu and Vāgīśvarī, and the final liberation of the bound soul (paśu) through the grace of Śiva.

beats.title: Vidyāviśodhanavidhāna: The Procedure for Purifying the Knowledge
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for purifying and establishing the vidyā (knowledge/mantra) in the context of nirvāṇadīkṣā (initiation into liberation). It details the sandhāna (union) of the vidyā with the prācīnakalā (ancient aspect), the enumeration of tattvas (principles), the mantra components, and the ritual actions including tāḍana (striking), chedana (cutting), praveśa (entry), and yojana (joining). The chapter also covers the purification of the body, senses, and tattvas, and concludes with offerings to Rudra.

beats.title: Śāntiśodhana in Nirvāṇadīkṣā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the process of śāntiśodhana (purification of peace) within the nirvāṇadīkṣā (liberation initiation). It details the merging of tattvas into śānti, the identification of rudras and their locations, the use of mantras and rituals to purify the bonds (pāśa) of the soul, and the final offering and dismissal of īśvara.

beats.title: Nirvāṇadīkṣā: The Liberation Initiation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of nirvāṇadīkṣā (initiation into liberation), focusing on the process of uniting the disciple's self with Śiva through mantras, mudras, and meditative practices. It details the purification of the disciple's bonds (pāśa), the offering of oblations, and the final merging of the individual self with the supreme consciousness.

beats.title: Ekatattva-dīkṣā (Initiation into the Single Principle)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ekatattva-dīkṣā (initiation into the single principle). It begins with a continuation from the previous chapter on nirvāṇa-dīkṣā, then presents the procedure for ekatattva-dīkṣā, including the mantra, offerings, and the role of the guru. The chapter emphasizes the unity of the self with Śiva and the importance of devotion.

beats.title: Abhiṣeka Ritual for Disciple
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of abhiṣeka (consecration) for a disciple, including the arrangement of nine pots representing the oceans and the eight Vidyeśvaras, the construction of an altar, and the procedures for purification, anointing, and dressing the disciple.

beats.title: Various Mantras and Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses various rituals and mantras for worship, including abhisheka (consecration) of deities like Shiva and Vishnu, the use of specific materials like ghee and sandalwood, and methods for divination using mantras and number patterns. It also covers the effects of such practices on the practitioner's spiritual and worldly life.

beats.title: The Ritual of Installation (Pratiṣṭhā)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 92) provides a detailed exposition of the ritual of installation (pratiṣṭhā) of a liṅga or image, including the selection and purification of the site, the preparation of the foundation, the consecration of the stones (śilā), and the invocation of deities and tattvas. It outlines the five types of installation, the examination of soil, the removal of impurities, and the placement of the śilās with appropriate mantras and offerings.

beats.title: Vastu Puja and Building Construction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions for the worship of Vastu Purusha, the deity of the site, including the layout of the Vastu mandala, placement of deities, offerings to various beings, and the construction of buildings. It describes the 81-pada (square) grid, the 12 vital points (marmans) to avoid, and the specific offerings to the 45 deities residing in the mandala. The chapter also covers variations for different types of structures like houses, temples, villages, and forts.

beats.title: The Placement of Stones in Temple Construction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of placing stones (śilāvinyāsa) in the construction of a temple or building. It details the worship of deities, the placement of stones in specific positions, and the recitation of mantras for each stone, invoking various goddesses like Nandā, Bhadrā, Jayā, and others. The procedure includes offerings, homa, and the installation of the central stone, with the goal of ensuring auspiciousness and removing obstacles.

beats.title: Pratiṣṭhā Sāmagrī Vidhāna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 95) provides detailed instructions for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of a liṅga in a temple, focusing on the collection of materials (sāmagrī) needed for the ritual. It covers auspicious times, dimensions of the maṇḍapa and kuṇḍa (fire altar), types of wood for toranas, colors of flags, and various items such as clay, herbs, grains, metals, gems, and ritual implements. The chapter emphasizes the importance of proper preparation for the ceremony to ensure success and spiritual benefits.

beats.title: Adhivāsanavidhiḥ: The Ritual of Consecration
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Adhivāsanavidhiḥ' (Ritual of Consecration), provides a detailed guide for the consecration of a Śiva liṅga. It covers purification rites, installation of deities and guardians, nyāsa (placement of mantras and energies), homa (fire offerings), and the final establishment of the liṅga. The text emphasizes the integration of tattvas (principles) and the role of the guru in ensuring the ritual's efficacy.

beats.title: Chapter 97
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 97) provides a detailed ritual manual for the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of a Śiva liṅga. It covers preliminary rites, placement of the liṅga, installation procedures, homa (fire offerings), and concluding ceremonies, including the establishment of attendant deities and the handling of various types of liṅgas.

beats.title: Gaurīpratiṣṭhākathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for establishing (pratiṣṭhā) the goddess Gaurī, including the construction of the maṇḍapa, installation of the image, placement of various śaktis (powers), and the specific mantras and offerings to be used.

beats.title: Establishment of the Sun God
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for establishing (pratishtha) an image of the Sun god (Surya). It details the preparatory rituals, the construction of a mandapa, the placement of the deity, the installation of mantras, and the purification processes.

beats.title: Door Installation Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for establishing a door (dvārapratiṣṭhā), including the purification of door components, placement of mantras, offerings, and homa.

beats.title: Temple Consecration (Prāsādapratiṣṭhā)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual for consecrating a temple (prāsādapratiṣṭhā). It details the placement of a pot (kumbha) with sacred substances, the infusion of life force (prāṇa) into the image through breath control and mantras, and the installation of various deities and energies. The process involves meditation on Rudra and Umā, and culminates in offerings and homa.

beats.title: The Ritual of Hoisting the Flag
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of hoisting a flag (dhvajāropaṇa) on a temple or shrine. It details the construction of the flagpole, the materials, the mantras, the placement of various deities and tattvas, and the benefits for the patron. The procedure involves purification, meditation, offerings, and homa to ensure auspiciousness.

beats.title: Procedure for Renovation of a Damaged Shiva Linga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the procedure for renovating or replacing a damaged or old Shiva linga (jīrṇoddhāra). It details the rituals, mantras, and offerings required to properly remove the old linga and install a new one, emphasizing the importance of following the prescribed rites to avoid negative consequences.

beats.title: Temple Architecture
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the general characteristics of temple architecture, including the layout of the sanctum, pillars, doors, and various types of temples classified by shape and style. It provides measurements and proportions for different parts of the temple, and warns against certain defects in construction.

beats.title: Nagaradi Vastu Kathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 105) provides detailed instructions on the layout and construction of towns, villages, forts, and houses according to Vastu principles. It describes the 81-pada (81-square) grid for worship, the placement of deities in various directions, and the dimensions and features of different types of buildings, including one, two, three, four, and eight-halled houses. The chapter also discusses the auspicious and inauspicious effects of various structural features and the placement of rooms for specific purposes.

beats.title: Instructions on City and House Construction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 106) provides detailed instructions on the construction of cities, towns, forts, and houses (nagarādivāstu). It covers the layout, placement of gates, allocation of quarters for different castes and professions, orientation of buildings, and the importance of dedicating structures to deities to avoid affliction by evil spirits. The chapter emphasizes that properly designed settlements bring victory, enjoyment, and liberation.

beats.title: Svāyambhuvasarga: The Creation of the World
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of the world according to the Svayambhuva Manvantara, detailing the distribution of the seven continents (dvīpas) by Priyavrata to his sons, the geography of Jambudvīpa with its eight subcontinents (varṣas), and the lineage of kings from Ṛṣabha to Satyajit. It also mentions the blissful nature of the subcontinents and the yogic attainment of Ṛṣabha.

beats.title: Cosmography of the Seven Continents
beats.summary: This chapter describes the geography of the seven continents (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, focusing on Jambūdvīpa and Mount Meru. It details the mountains, rivers, forests, and regions within Jambūdvīpa, including the nine varṣas (regions) and the locations of various celestial beings and lokapālas.

beats.title: Glory of Tirthas
beats.summary: Agni describes the glory of sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites), emphasizing the importance of self-control, fasting, charity, and visiting tirthas. He lists numerous tirthas across India, including Pushkara, Kurukshetra, Varanasi, Prayaga, and many others, and declares that bathing, giving donations, or even residing there grants liberation and the fruits of sacrifices.

beats.title: The Glory of the Ganga
beats.summary: Agni describes the glory of the Ganga, stating that she grants both enjoyment and liberation, and that the lands through which she flows become pure and excellent.

beats.title: The Greatness of Prayaga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the greatness of Prayaga (the confluence of Ganga and Yamuna) as a sacred place. It explains that bathing, giving charity, performing shraddha, and dying there grant liberation and heavenly rewards. Various tirthas within Prayaga are enumerated, and the chapter emphasizes the supreme merit of Prayaga over other holy sites.

beats.title: The Glory of Varanasi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the glory of Varanasi (Kashi) as narrated by Lord Shiva to Goddess Gauri. It highlights Varanasi as the supreme holy place, bestowing both worldly enjoyment and liberation. The chapter lists eight secret sacred sites within Avimukta (Varanasi), explains the etymology of Varanasi as the land between the rivers Varana and Asi, and declares that all religious acts performed there yield liberation.

beats.title: Glory of Narmada and Amarakaṇṭaka
beats.summary: Agni describes the glory of the Narmada river and the sacred site of Amarakaṇṭaka, including the confluence with the Kāverī, the Śrīparvata mountain, and the benefits of pilgrimage, charity, and penance there.

beats.title: The Glory of Gayā-tīrtha
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the glory of Gayā-tīrtha. It describes how the demon Gayāsura performed severe penance, causing distress to the gods. Viṣṇu granted him a boon that he would become the holiest of all tīrthas. Later, Brahmā requested Gayāsura's body for a sacrifice, and the demon agreed. During the sacrifice, Gayāsura began to move, so Viṣṇu placed a divine stone (śilā) on him to keep him steady. The stone became the sacred Gayā-śilā, where Viṣṇu as Gadādhara resides. The chapter also tells the story of Dharmavratā, who was cursed to become a stone but later blessed to be the divine stone. Finally, it describes the establishment of Gayā-kṣetra and the benefits of performing rites there.

beats.title: The Pilgrimage to Gayā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the pilgrimage to Gayā, detailing the rituals, tīrthas (sacred sites), and the benefits of performing śrāddha and piṇḍadāna there. It emphasizes that a son's pilgrimage to Gayā liberates ancestors from hell and grants them heaven or mokṣa. The chapter includes instructions for bathing, offerings, and mantras, along with a story of King Viśāla who attained liberation through piṇḍadāna.

beats.title: Gayāyātrāvidhi: The Procedure of the Gayā Pilgrimage
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Gayā pilgrimage (Gayāyātrā) rituals, including bathing, sandhyā worship, and offering piṇḍa (rice balls) at various sacred sites in Gayā. It lists numerous tīrthas (holy spots) and deities to be worshipped, emphasizing the benefits of performing these rites for the liberation of ancestors and oneself from the three debts. The chapter concludes with a hymn to Gadādhara (Viṣṇu) and a statement on the merit of reciting the Gayā Māhātmya.

beats.title: Śrāddha Ritual Manual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Śrāddhakalpaḥ', provides a detailed manual for performing śrāddha rituals, including the selection of Brahmins, the procedure for offerings to ancestors (pitṛs), the use of mantras, and the types of food that grant specific durations of satisfaction. It also covers special śrāddhas like ekoddiṣṭa, sapiṇḍīkaraṇa, and ābhyudayika, along with the benefits of performing śrāddha on specific lunar days.

beats.title: Description of Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the land of Bharata (India) as a karmabhumi, the land of action, situated north of the ocean and south of the Himalayas, extending nine thousand yojanas. It is the place where beings can attain heaven or liberation through their actions.

beats.title: Description of the Continents and Oceans
beats.summary: This chapter describes the geography of the seven continents (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, as narrated by Agni. It details Jambūdvīpa, Plakṣadvīpa, Śālmala, Krauñca, Śāka, and Puṣkara, including their dimensions, rulers, mountains, rivers, and inhabitants. The chapter also mentions the Lokāloka mountain and the cosmic egg.

beats.title: Bhuvanakosha: Cosmology of the Universe
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmology of the universe according to the Agni Purana, including the dimensions of the earth, the seven netherworlds (patalas), the planetary system, the sun's chariot, the moon's chariot, and the ultimate reality as Vishnu. It also explains the relationship between Vishnu and the universe, emphasizing that all existence is a manifestation of Vishnu's power.

beats.title: Jyotisha Shastra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 121) is a treatise on Jyotisha (astrology), detailing auspicious and inauspicious timings for various life events such as marriage, upanayana, house construction, agriculture, and more. It includes rules based on nakshatras, tithis, planetary positions, and other astrological factors.

beats.title: Kāla Gaṇana: The Computation of Time
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a detailed method for calculating time (kāla gaṇana) based on astronomical cycles, including tithis, nakṣatras, and planetary periods. It provides formulas for determining lunar days, asterisms, and corrections for ṛṇa (deficit) and dhana (excess). The text is highly technical, focusing on computational rules for calendrical astronomy.

beats.title: Nānāyogāḥ (Various Yogas)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 123) is titled 'Nānāyogāḥ' (Various Yogas) and focuses on astrological and ritual practices for victory in battle. It describes the calculation of time units (nāḍī, pala, liptā), the effects of planetary positions (Śani, Rāhu), the Śanicakra, Kūrmacakra, Rāhucakra, and the classification of muhūrtas (auspicious moments) for various activities. It also provides mantras and recipes for tilaka (forehead mark) and lepa (ointment) to achieve control over others, including gods and humans.

beats.title: Yuddhajayārṇavīyajyotiḥśāstrasāra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yuddhajayārṇavīyajyotiḥśāstrasāra', presents a discourse on the essence of astrology and mantra for victory in battle. It begins with Agni speaking to the sage, then the Devi asks how the gods defeated the demons. Ishvara explains the origin of the fifteen-syllable Shakti mantra, the five primary mantras (Rig, Yajur, Sama, Atharva, and the Sadyojata group), and the phonemes (a, i, u, e, o) as the root of all. He describes the subtle anatomy of sound, the chakras, and the process of meditation on the white form emitting nectar for immortality and victory. The chapter concludes with a method for applying mantras on the body and the assurance of victory in battle.

beats.title: Yuddhajayārṇavīyanānācakrāṇi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yuddhajayārṇavīyanānācakrāṇi' (Various Wheels for Victory in War), is a discourse by Lord Śiva on occult and astrological techniques to ensure victory in battle. It includes mantras, deity invocations, cakra (wheel) systems, tithi-yoga combinations, planetary influences, and rituals such as homa (fire offerings) using gruesome materials. The chapter also describes protective herbs and the use of Hanumān's image to break enemy formations.

beats.title: Nakshatra Nirnaya: Determination of Auspicious Times
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 126) provides a detailed astrological guide for determining auspicious and inauspicious times based on nakshatras (lunar mansions), planetary positions, and other celestial factors. It covers topics such as the effects of the Sun and Rahu in various nakshatras, classification of nakshatras into fixed, movable, mild, and fierce types, and rules for selecting favorable times for activities like travel, construction, and warfare. The chapter also discusses 'dagdha' (burnt) tithis and 'tripushkara' yogas, which are considered inauspicious.

beats.title: Nānābalāni: Various Strengths and Astrological Considerations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses astrological considerations for determining auspicious and inauspicious times, particularly for activities like travel, war, and marriage. It covers the effects of planetary positions, lunar mansions (nakshatras), and the characteristics of zodiac signs (fixed, movable, dual). The chapter also includes rules for avoiding certain muhurtas (auspicious moments) based on the yoga (combination) and planetary placements.

beats.title: The Koṭacakra: A Diagram for Victory
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the construction and interpretation of the Koṭacakra, a diagram used for determining auspiciousness in warfare and other endeavors. It details the placement of nakshatras (lunar mansions) in concentric rings and the central area, and explains how the positions of planets and nakshatras influence victory or defeat.

beats.title: Arghakāṇḍa: The Ritual Offering
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the arghakāṇḍa, a ritual offering based on lunar months and omens such as meteors, earthquakes, eclipses, and directional fires. It specifies the types of offerings (grains, ghee, cloth, etc.) to be made in each month to achieve prosperity.

beats.title: Ghātacakra: The Circle of Destruction
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Ghātacakra, a system of astrological circles (maṇḍalas) used for predicting victory or defeat based on planetary and celestial omens. It details the Āgneya maṇḍala (fire circle) and its associated portents, such as earthquakes, eclipses, comets, and unusual weather, which indicate destruction of specific regions and suffering for the populace. The chapter also mentions the Vāyavya maṇḍala (wind circle) and provides rules for interpreting lunar positions in relation to asterisms.

beats.title: Ghātacakrādi: On Circles of Killing and Victory
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 131) describes various astrological and numerological methods for predicting victory or defeat in battle, including the construction and interpretation of cakras (circles) based on syllables, tithis, and nakshatras. It details the ghātacakra (killing circle), naracakra (human circle), and jayacakra (victory circle), providing rules for determining auspicious and inauspicious outcomes based on the positions of planets and the length of names.

beats.title: Sevācakra: The Wheel of Service
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Sevācakra (Service Wheel), a divinatory system for assessing gain and loss in relationships. It includes instructions for constructing a grid of 35 cells, assigning letters and categories (deities, demons, etc.), and interpreting results based on planetary positions, star constellations (Tārācakra), and zodiac signs. The chapter also discusses friendship and enmity between signs.

beats.title: Nānābalāni (Various Armies)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Nānābalāni' (Various Armies), provides astrological and ritual instructions for achieving victory in battle. It describes the characteristics of individuals born under different planetary influences, the effects of planetary periods (daśā), and various mantras, yantras, and practices such as the Tārkṣya (Garuda) cakra, Picchikā, and Mṛtyuñjaya for overcoming enemies and ensuring success in warfare.

beats.title: Trailokyavijaya Vidya
beats.summary: Lord Shiva describes the Trailokyavijaya Vidya, a powerful mantra and ritual for overcoming obstacles, subduing enemies, and gaining control over the three worlds. The chapter includes the mantra, visualization of the goddess, and instructions for nyasa and homa.

beats.title: Saṅgrāmavijayavidyā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents the Saṅgrāmavijayavidyā, a mantra and ritual practice for victory in battle. It includes a long invocation to the goddess Cāmuṇḍā, describing her fearsome form and powers, and prescribes the visualization of a 28-armed deity. The practice involves recitation, homa (fire sacrifice), and other rites to ensure success in war.

beats.title: The Nakshatra Chakra
beats.summary: Lord Shiva describes the Nakshatra Chakra, a wheel of 27 lunar mansions divided into three groups (nāḍīs) for determining auspiciousness in travel and other undertakings. The placement of planets in these nāḍīs indicates favorable or unfavorable results.

beats.title: Mahamari Vidya: The Great Destroyer
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Mahamari Vidya, a powerful mantra and ritual for destroying enemies. It includes the mantra, the iconography of the goddess Mahamari, and various homa (fire offerings) for specific effects such as causing disease, death, or destruction of enemy armies. The chapter also outlines a battlefield ritual involving a cloth painting and maidens.

beats.title: Ṣaṭkarmāṇi: The Six Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Ṣaṭkarmāṇi' (Six Rites), describes various mantra-based rituals and their applications. It details the construction of mantra syllables for purposes such as attraction, subjugation, pacification, and destruction, along with associated deities and practices.

beats.title: The Sixty-Year Cycle of Jupiter
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the sixty-year cycle of Jupiter (Brihaspati) and the characteristics of each year in terms of agriculture, prosperity, health, and other omens. It is a continuation of the Yuddhajayārṇava section, focusing on the auspicious and inauspicious effects of each samvatsara.

beats.title: Vaśyādiyogāḥ: Practices for Subjugation and Attraction
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various yogic practices (vaśyādiyogāḥ) involving the use of herbs, mantras, and rituals for purposes such as subjugation, attraction, and purification. It provides detailed instructions on preparing unguents, incense, and pills using specific plants and astrological combinations, with applications for controlling others, enhancing beauty, and achieving various worldly goals.

beats.title: The Knowledge of the Thirty-Six Herbs
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the knowledge of the thirty-six herbs (ṣaṭtriṃśatpadaka) and their preparations for achieving immortality and curing all diseases. It lists the herbs, their combinations, dosages, and the benefits of using them in various forms like powder, pill, decoction, etc. The chapter also specifies the number of herbs to be used for curing specific ailments and emphasizes that this knowledge should not be given to just anyone.

beats.title: Mantras, Herbs, and Astrological Wheels
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various astrological and occult techniques, including the use of mantras and herbs, the calculation of planetary influences (especially Saturn and Rahu), and methods to determine auspicious and inauspicious times for actions such as war. It provides detailed rules for interpreting names, planetary positions, and tithis to predict outcomes.

beats.title: Kubjika Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of Kubjika, a goddess, including the placement of mantras, the nyasa of deities on the body, the arrangement of a mandala with various goddesses and pithas, and the five Vimalas. It provides a ritual procedure for achieving all aims.

beats.title: Kubjika Puja
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of the goddess Kubjika, including mantras, rituals, and the iconography of the deity. It details the puja procedure, the arrangement of the mandala, and the various deities and siddhas to be worshipped.

beats.title: Mālinī Mantras and Nyāsa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the various mantras and nyāsa (ritual placement) practices associated with the goddess Kubjikā and the Mālinī tradition. It details the placement of deities and goddesses on different parts of the body, including the names and positions of the Mālinī mantra syllables and associated deities.

beats.title: The Trikhaṇḍī Mantra and the Eight Groups of Goddesses
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Trikhaṇḍī mantra and the worship of the eight groups of goddesses (Aṣṭāṣṭakadevyaḥ). It provides the mantra syllables, their arrangement, and the associated deities for each part of the body, focusing on the esoteric practice of mantra nyāsa.

beats.title: The Worship of Tvarita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of the goddess Tvarita, including her mantra, nyasa, and the nine shaktis. It provides detailed instructions for the ritual, including the bija mantras, the placement of the goddess's limbs (anganyasa), and the names of her attendant deities.

beats.title: Ritual for Victory in Battle
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes a ritual for victory in battle, focusing on the worship of the Sun god (Surya) with specific mantras and offerings. It includes instructions for a six-limbed worship (ṣaḍaṅga) and mentions various deities and concepts associated with the ritual.

beats.title: The Lakh and Crore Homa for Victory
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of performing a lakh (100,000) or crore (10 million) homa (fire sacrifice) to ensure victory in battle, attainment of kingdom, and destruction of obstacles. It details the preparatory purifications, diet restrictions, and the immense benefits including protection from enemies, diseases, natural calamities, and even attaining heaven with the physical body.

beats.title: The Fourteen Manvantaras
beats.summary: This chapter enumerates the fourteen Manvantaras, listing the Manu, Indra, gods, seven sages, and sons for each period. It also describes the division of the Veda by Vyasa at the end of Dvapara Yuga.

beats.title: Varṇetaradharmāḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Varṇetaradharmāḥ', discusses the duties and characteristics of different varṇas (social classes) and mixed castes, as well as general virtues applicable to all. It is narrated by Puṣkara to Paraśurāma, recounting teachings from Varuṇa. The chapter covers sāmānya dharma (common duties), specific duties for brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas, vaiśyas, and śūdras, and the origins of various mixed castes through anuloma and pratiloma marriages.

beats.title: Duties of a Householder
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the duties and permissible occupations for a Brahmana in the householder stage (gṛhastha). It states that a Brahmana should live by his own prescribed occupation, but if necessary, may adopt the occupations of Kshatriya, Vaishya, or Shudra, except for certain prohibited activities like selling milk products, salt, lac, and meat. The chapter also mentions that a Brahmana may engage in agriculture, trade, cattle-rearing, and usury, but must avoid certain items.

beats.title: Brahmacaryāśramadharmaḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Brahmacaryāśramadharmaḥ', outlines the duties and rituals of the brahmacarya (student) stage of life, including rules for marriage, naming ceremonies, upanayana (sacred thread ceremony), and daily practices such as eating and offering oblations.

beats.title: Marriage (Vivāha)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the rules and rituals of marriage (vivāha). It covers the number of wives permissible for different castes, the importance of marrying within one's caste, the eight types of marriage (Brahma, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, Paishacha, and Brahma again?), auspicious times and constellations for marriage, and prohibitions regarding certain days and planetary positions.

beats.title: Ācāra (Conduct)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Ācāra' (Conduct), prescribes daily rituals and ethical behaviors for a householder. It covers morning routines, bathing procedures, proper conduct towards others, and prohibitions against various impure or disrespectful actions.

beats.title: Dravyaśuddhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Dravyaśuddhi' (Purification of Substances), provides detailed rules for purifying various materials and objects through methods such as reheating, washing, scrubbing, and sprinkling water. It also covers purification of animals and individuals after certain activities.

beats.title: Śāvāśaucādiḥ: Rules of Purification After Death and Childbirth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Śāvāśaucādiḥ', discusses rules of purification after death (pretaśuddhi) and childbirth (sūtikāśuddhi). It prescribes varying periods of impurity for different castes, procedures for offerings to the deceased, and exceptions for certain types of death. The sage Puṣkara instructs Paraśurāma on these rites.

beats.title: Śāvāśauca and Sūtaka: Rules of Impurity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 158) provides detailed rules for śāvāśauca (death impurity) and sūtaka (birth impurity) for different varṇas, ages, and relationships. It covers durations of impurity, procedures for cremation, bone collection, and rituals for the deceased, including exceptions for special circumstances like suicide, epidemics, and war.

beats.title: Purification and the Afterlife
beats.summary: This chapter discusses purification rituals (śauca) for the dead, especially the importance of immersing bones in the Ganges for liberation, the role of Nārāyaṇa bali for fallen souls, and the urgency of performing righteous deeds before death.

beats.title: Vānaprastha Āśrama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the duties and lifestyle of a vānaprastha (forest-dwelling hermit), as taught by Puṣkara. It includes practices such as matted hair, fire rituals, sleeping on the ground, wearing deer skin, living in the forest, subsisting on roots and fruits, abstaining from accepting gifts, bathing three times daily, celibacy, and worship of gods and guests.

beats.title: Yatirdharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yatirdharma', describes the duties and practices of a renunciant (yati). It covers the stages of life, the conduct of a sannyasi, the types of alms, the qualities of a true renunciant, the practice of yoga (including pranayama, pratyahara, dhyana, dharana, samadhi), and the realization of the self as Brahman. The chapter emphasizes detachment, non-violence, truthfulness, and the pursuit of liberation.

beats.title: Dharmaśāstra Discourse
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses dharmaśāstra, listing various lawgivers and explaining the two types of Vedic karma (pravṛtta and nivṛtta). It emphasizes the importance of self-knowledge (ātmajñāna) as the highest path, and details rules for Vedic study, including times of adhyāya (non-study) due to impurities or inauspicious events.

beats.title: Śrāddha Kalpa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Śrāddha Kalpa, the ritual procedure for performing śrāddha (offerings to ancestors). It details the steps including inviting Brahmins, seating them, offering arghya, invoking the Viśvedevas and Pitṛs, making offerings in fire, feeding the Brahmins, offering piṇḍas (rice balls) to the ancestors, and the subsequent rites. It also specifies the types of food and offerings that yield various benefits, and the auspicious times for performing śrāddha.

beats.title: Navagraha Homa
beats.summary: This chapter describes the navagraha homa (sacrifice to the nine planets) as taught by Puṣkara. It details the materials, mantras, offerings, and procedures for performing the ritual to fulfill various desires such as wealth, peace, rain, longevity, and progeny.

beats.title: Nānādharmāḥ (Various Dharmas)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses various dharmas (duties), including meditation on the self, rules for śrāddha offerings, purification of women, the nature of yoga, and the importance of meditation for cleansing sins.

beats.title: The Fivefold Dharma and Sacraments
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, narrated by Puṣkara, discusses the fivefold classification of dharma based on varṇa (caste) and āśrama (stage of life). It details the various saṃskāras (sacraments), yajñas (sacrifices), and ethical practices that lead to liberation. The chapter also includes rules for conduct, such as silence during certain activities, and prohibitions like not accepting flowers after bathing. It concludes with instructions for funeral rites for unrelated persons.

beats.title: The Planetary Sacrifice for Peace and Victory
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 167 describes the Graha Yajna (planetary sacrifice) for achieving peace, victory, and other desires. It details the construction of fire altars, placement of planetary deities, offerings, homas (fire oblations) of varying scales (ayuta, laksha, koti), and associated rituals including abhisheka (consecration) and dana (donations). The chapter also covers vidveshana (enmity) and abhicara (sorcery) rites using triangular altars.

beats.title: Mahāpātakādikathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses various sins (mahāpātaka, upapātaka, etc.) and their corresponding expiatory rites (prāyaścitta), including rules about forbidden food, contact with outcastes, and specific penances like kṛcchra and cāndrāyaṇa.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites for Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 169) prescribes expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for various sins, including mahāpātaka (great sins) and upapātaka (minor sins). It details penances for brahmahatyā (killing a brāhmaṇa), goghna (killing a cow), surāpāna (drinking liquor), suvarṇasteya (stealing gold), gurutalpa (incest with guru's wife), and other offenses. The chapter also covers proportional penances for killing beings of different varṇas and animals, and specifies rites for purification.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites (Prāyaścittāni)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 170) prescribes various expiatory rites (prāyaścittas) for different sins and impurities, including association with fallen persons, contact with outcastes, improper eating, and other violations of dharma. It details specific penances such as fasting, recitation of Gāyatrī, and performance of kṛcchra vows for purification.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for purification from sins, including recitation of hymns, fasting, and specific penances like kṛcchra and cāndrāyaṇa.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites and the Viṣṇu Stotra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for sins, including fasting regimens (kṛcchra) and a hymn to Viṣṇu for the destruction of all sins. The sage Puṣkara explains that when the mind inclines toward sins like adultery, theft, or violence, the appropriate expiation is recitation of a stotra praising Viṣṇu. The chapter emphasizes that chanting Viṣṇu's names and meditating on him purifies all sins committed by body, mind, or speech.

beats.title: Expiation for Sins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 173) deals with expiation (prāyaścitta) for various sins, primarily focusing on the killing of a Brahmin (brahmahatyā), but also covering other sins such as killing of other beings, theft, adultery, and consumption of forbidden substances. It prescribes specific penances, including vows, fasting, recitations, and gifts, varying according to the caste of the victim and the intent of the sinner.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites (Prāyaścitta)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses various expiatory rites (prāyaścitta) for lapses in worship, including rules for atonement when rituals are omitted or performed incorrectly. It emphasizes the power of remembering Hari (Viṣṇu) as the ultimate expiation, and describes mantras, vows, and donations that destroy sins. The chapter also identifies Agni as a form of Viṣṇu, highlighting the unity of the divine.

beats.title: Vrataparibhāṣā: The Science of Vows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Vrataparibhāṣā' (Definitions of Vows), provides a comprehensive guide on the rules and classifications of religious vows (vrata), including definitions of vrata, tapas, upavāsa, and various types of kṛcchra and cāndrāyaṇa penances. It details permissible and forbidden foods during vows, the importance of tithis and nakṣatras, procedures for expiating broken vows, and the benefits of observing vows. The chapter also includes mantras for vow initiation and worship of Keśava.

beats.title: Vratas Beginning on Pratipad
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) starting from the first day (pratipad) of lunar months. It details the worship of Brahma and Agni, including rituals, offerings, and the benefits of performing these vratas, such as attaining heaven, wealth, and liberation.

beats.title: Vratas on Dvitīyā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various vratas (vows) observed on the second day (dvitīyā) of different lunar fortnights. It covers the Dvitīyāvrata, Aśūnyaśayana vrāta, Kāntivrata, and Śiṣṇuvrata, detailing the rituals, deities to worship, and the benefits such as beauty, health, wealth, and liberation. The chapter emphasizes the importance of fasting, offerings, and charity.

beats.title: Third Vows (Tṛtīyāvratāni)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the third set of vows (tṛtīyāvratāni), focusing on the Mūlagaurīvrata and other observances performed on the third lunar day (tṛtīyā) of bright fortnights. It details rituals for worshipping Umā-Maheśvara (Śiva and Pārvatī) with specific offerings, mantras, and pūjā procedures, promising worldly enjoyment (bhukti) and liberation (mukti). The chapter also lists various names of the goddess and prescribes seasonal variations.

beats.title: Caturthi Vows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various Caturthi (fourth lunar day) vows, including the procedure for worshiping Ganesha (the elephant-headed deity) to remove obstacles and grant worldly enjoyment and liberation. It specifies the mantras, offerings, and timing for different months.

beats.title: Pañcamīvrata (Vow of the Fifth Lunar Day)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Pañcamīvrata (vow of the fifth lunar day), which grants health, heaven, and liberation. It lists the serpent kings (Nāgas) to be worshipped on the fifth day of the bright fortnight in the months of Nabhas (Śrāvaṇa), Nabhasya (Bhādrapada), Aśvina, and Kārttika. The serpents include Vāsuki, Takṣaka, Kālīya, Maṇibhadra, Airāvata, Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Karkoṭaka, and Dhanañjaya. Worship of these serpents bestows fearlessness, longevity, knowledge, fame, and prosperity.

beats.title: The Vows of the Sixth Lunar Day
beats.summary: Agni reveals the vows of the sixth lunar day (ṣaṣṭhīvrata), including the Skanda-ṣaṣṭhī vow in Bhādrapada and the Kṛṣṇa-ṣaṣṭhī vow in Mārgaśīrṣa, which grant enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Saptamī Vratas
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 182 describes the vratas (vows) associated with the seventh lunar day (saptamī) in various months, including their benefits such as liberation (mukti) and enjoyment (bhukti). The chapter is narrated by Agni and provides specific rituals for worshipping the Sun god (Arka) on these days.

beats.title: Jayanti Ashtami Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Jayanti Ashtami vrata (fast) observed on the eighth lunar day of the dark fortnight in the month of Bhadrapada, when the moon is in the Rohini nakshatra.
