It details the ritual worship of Krishna, Balarama, Devaki, Vasudeva, and others, along with mantras and offerings, promising liberation and fulfillment of desires.

beats.title: The Story of Dhīra and the Aṣṭamī Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various aṣṭamī vratas (eighth-day observances) dedicated to Śiva, including the kṛṣṇāṣṭamī vrata performed over a year with specific monthly rituals, and the budhavāra aṣṭamī vrata. It also narrates a story about a brāhmaṇa named Dhīra and his family, who through observance of aṣṭamī vrata attain heavenly rewards.

beats.title: Navami Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Navami (ninth day) vratas, including the worship of the goddess Durga in various forms, the associated mantras, rituals, and the benefits of observing these vows. It details the iconography of the goddess, the procedure for worship, and the offerings to be made.

beats.title: Daśamīvrata
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 186 describes the daśamīvrata (tenth-day vow), which grants dharma, kāma, and other desires. The procedure involves eating once on the tenth day, donating ten cows at the end, and offering golden replicas of the directions, leading to becoming a lord of Brahmins.

beats.title: Ekadashi Vrata
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 187 describes the Ekadashi vow (ekādaśīvrata), its rules, benefits, and special combinations with lunar asterisms. It emphasizes fasting on Ekadashi, avoiding certain foods and activities, and the importance of breaking the fast on Dwadashi. Specific auspicious timings like Ekadashi coinciding with Pushya nakshatra or Shravana nakshatra are highlighted for greater merit.

beats.title: Various Dvādaśī Vows
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 188 describes various dvādaśī (twelfth lunar day) vows, including Madana, Bhīma, Govinda, Viśoka, Tiladvādaśī, and others, with specific rituals, mantras, and fruits such as wealth, progeny, heaven, and liberation.

beats.title: Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Śravaṇadvādaśīvrata, a vow observed on the twelfth lunar day of the bright fortnight in the month of Bhādrapada, when the Śravaṇa nakṣatra is present. The ritual includes bathing at a confluence, fasting, worship of Viṣṇu as Vāmana, offering arghya, homa, and pāraṇa on the thirteenth day. The vow is said to grant bhukti (enjoyment) and mukti (liberation).

beats.title: Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Akhaṇḍa Dvādaśī Vrata (unbroken twelfth-day vow), its procedure, and benefits. It is narrated by Agni. The vow involves fasting on the twelfth day of the bright fortnight in Mārgaśīrṣa, bathing with pañcagavya, worshipping Viṣṇu, and offering a pot filled with barley and rice to a brāhmaṇa. The devotee prays for the vow to be unbroken across seven births, and the chapter also mentions similar observances for other months and the Cāturmāsya vow.

beats.title: Trayodashi Vows
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Ananga Trayodashi and other Trayodashi vows, detailing the specific rituals, offerings, and deities to be worshipped on each month's Trayodashi day for various benefits.

beats.title: Caturdaśī Vratas (Fourteenth Day Observances)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) to be performed on the fourteenth day (caturdaśī) of different lunar months, primarily dedicated to Lord Shiva and Lord Vishnu (Ananta). It details specific rituals, offerings, and benefits such as worldly enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Śivarātri Vrata
beats.summary: Agni describes the Śivarātri vrata, a fast observed on the fourteenth day of the dark fortnight between Māgha and Phālguna, involving fasting, vigil, and worship of Śiva for worldly enjoyment and liberation. The story of the hunter Sundarasena illustrates the merit of this vrata.

beats.title: Vratas for Bhukti and Mukti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various vratas (religious observances) including the Aśokapūrṇimāvrata, Vṛṣotsarga, Pitryā vrata, and Sāvitryamāvāsyāvrata, detailing their procedures and the spiritual benefits such as bhukti (enjoyment) and mukti (liberation).

beats.title: Vāravratāni: Weekly Observances
beats.summary: Agni reveals the rules and benefits of various vāravratas (weekly observances) associated with specific lunar asterisms and weekdays, promising worldly enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Nakshatra Vows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various nakshatra (lunar mansion) vows (vratas) for worshiping Vishnu (Hari) to attain worldly benefits and liberation. It details the procedure for the Nakshatrapurusha vow, the Shambhavayaniya vow, and the Ananta vow, including specific offerings, mantras, and the story of King Mandhata.

beats.title: Day Vows (Divasavratani)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various day-long and multi-day vows (vratas) involving fasting, offerings, and worship of Vishnu, with specific rules for different lunar days and months, promising rewards such as wealth, heavenly realms, and liberation.

beats.title: Monthly Vows (Māsavratāni)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various monthly vows (māsavratas) that grant worldly enjoyment and liberation. It specifies observances for months like Āṣāḍha, Śrāvaṇa, Vaiśākha, Māgha, Caitra, Mārgaśīrṣa, and Āśvina, including fasting, abstaining from certain foods, and worship of Viṣṇu. The chapter also details the Kaumuda vow and the benefits of different practices such as ekāntaropavāsa, cāndrāyaṇa, and prājāpatya vrata.

beats.title: Various Vows
beats.summary: Agni describes various vows (vratas) that grant enjoyment and liberation, including seasonal vows, offerings of ghee, sesame, and cloth, bathing with five nectars, fasting on Ekadashi, and vows for women to worship Uma and Maheshvara for auspiciousness.

beats.title: The Vow of Lamp-Giving (Dīpadānavrata)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the 'Dīpadānavrata' (vow of lamp-giving), narrated by Agni. It explains the merits of offering lamps to Vishnu, especially during Karttika, and includes the story of Lalita, a princess who gave a thousand lamps and attained a blessed birth. The chapter warns against stealing lamps, leading to hell, and emphasizes the power of lamp-giving over other vows.

beats.title: Navavyūhārcana: Worship of the Nine Emanations
beats.summary: Agni describes the worship of the nine vyūhas (emanations) of Viṣṇu, including the arrangement of the maṇḍala, placement of deities, mantras, and the process of initiation (dīkṣā) for disciples.

beats.title: Puṣpādhyāyakathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Puṣpādhyāyakathana' (Discourse on Flowers), describes the types of flowers and other offerings that please Lord Hari (Viṣṇu), as well as those that are unsuitable. It also enumerates eight 'inner flowers' (bhāvapuṣpa) representing virtues like non-violence and truth, which are superior to physical offerings. The chapter concludes by categorizing offerings according to the five elements.

beats.title: Description of Hells
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 203 describes the various hells (narakas) that sinners are subjected to after death, based on their specific misdeeds. It also mentions that those who perform virtuous acts like worship of Vishnu with flowers, fasting on Ekadashi, or observing the Bhishma Panchaka vow avoid these hells.

beats.title: The Vow of Month-Long Fasting
beats.summary: Agni reveals the supreme vow of month-long fasting (māsopavāsa) dedicated to Viṣṇu, detailing its rules, benefits, and the procedure for completion, including donations and the attainment of Viṣṇu's abode.

beats.title: Bhishmapanchaka Vrata
beats.summary: Agni describes the Bhishmapanchaka vow, a five-day observance during the bright fortnight of Karttika starting from Ekadashi, involving thrice-daily baths, offerings of rice and sesame, tarpaṇa to deities and ancestors, silence, worship of Vishnu with pañcagavya and pañcāmṛta, anointing with sandal, burning ghee-mixed guggulu, lamp offering day and night, food offering, and japa of the mantra 'Oṃ namo vāsudevāya' 108 times.

beats.title: The Ritual of Offering Arghya to Agastya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of offering arghya to the sage Agastya, including the procedure, mantras, and benefits. It also recounts the story of Agastya's deeds such as consuming the demons Atapi and Vatapi, and drying up the ocean.

beats.title: Kaumuda Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Kaumuda vrata, a vow to be observed during the bright fortnight of the month of Ashvina, involving worship of Vishnu with offerings and fasting on Ekadashi, culminating in a ritual on Dwadashi.

beats.title: Vratadānasamuccaya
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 208, titled 'Vratadānasamuccaya' (Compilation of Vows and Gifts), presents a discourse by Agni on the general principles of vows and donations. It explains that all vows, gifts, and rituals are ultimately dedicated to Viṣṇu, as all deities are his manifestations. The chapter includes a detailed pūjā (worship) procedure and a formula for making donations, emphasizing the universal efficacy of such practices when performed with devotion. It concludes by stating that reciting or hearing this compilation grants all desires and liberation.

beats.title: Dāna Paribhāṣā (Rules of Charity)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the rules and classifications of charity (dāna), including the distinction between iṣṭa (sacrificial) and pūrta (public works) charity, auspicious times for giving, the qualities of a worthy recipient, the types of gifts, and the spiritual benefits. It also covers the proper procedures for giving and receiving, the deities associated with various items, and the importance of faith and intention.

beats.title: Mahādānas and Meru Dānas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the sixteen great gifts (mahādānas) and the ten Meru gifts, along with detailed instructions for constructing and offering various symbolic cows (dhenus) made of substances like jaggery, ghee, sesame, etc. It emphasizes the spiritual benefits of these gifts, including liberation and worldly enjoyment.

beats.title: The Merits of Various Donations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various types of donations (dāna) and their spiritual benefits, including giving cows, buffaloes, bulls, land, gold, sesame, food, water, umbrellas, shoes, lamps, and even knowledge (such as scriptures). It emphasizes that donating food is supreme, and that donating land, gold, and cows yields results for seven lifetimes. The chapter also prescribes rituals for donating a bull to free a deceased person from hell, and describes the iconography of Kālapuruṣa (Yama) for certain donations.

beats.title: Meru Donations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various meru (mountain-shaped) donations (merudāna) to be performed during specific months, along with their associated fruits. It details the construction and gifting of different types of merus made from gold, gems, grains, sesame, etc., and emphasizes the worship of deities like Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. The chapter concludes with a mantra for the meru donation.

beats.title: Pṛthvīdāna: The Donation of Land
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of donating land (pṛthvīdāna), including the classification of land into three types (uttamā, madhyamā, kanyasā), the construction of a golden kalpa tree, and the merits of donating land, cows, and villages. It also mentions the donation of a cow (kāmadhenu) and the release of a bull (vṛṣotsarga).

beats.title: The Greatness of Mantra: Inner Anatomy and Prāṇāyāma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana explains the esoteric anatomy of the subtle body, focusing on the nāḍīcakra (wheel of nadis), the ten vital airs (prāṇa, apāna, etc.), and the practice of prāṇāyāma. It describes the locations of deities in the body, the ajapā gāyatrī mantra, and the three types of prāsāda (mantra recitation). The chapter emphasizes that knowledge of these inner processes leads to liberation.

beats.title: The Procedure of Sandhyā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Sandhyāvidhiḥ' (The Procedure of Sandhyā), is a discourse by Agni on the significance and practice of the Gāyatrī mantra. It details the mantra's structure, the deities associated with its syllables, the benefits of japa (chanting) with various counts, the use of different offerings in homa (fire sacrifice) for specific desires, and the procedure for nyāsa (ritual placement) and meditation. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of Gāyatrī, capable of absolving even great sins like brahmahatyā (killing a brāhmaṇa) when chanted a lakh times.

beats.title: The Nirvāṇa of Gāyatrī
beats.summary: Agni reveals the method of performing sandhyā and japa of Gāyatrī mantra, explaining its names (Gāyatrī, Sāvitrī, Sarasvatī) and the meaning of its components (bhargas, vareṇya, etc.), identifying the supreme light with Viṣṇu, and noting that different traditions worship it as Śiva, Śakti, Sūrya, or Agni.

beats.title: Gayatri Nirvana: The Liberation through Gayatri
beats.summary: Agni describes how Vasistha attained liberation by praising the linga form of Shiva and practicing Gayatri yoga. The chapter contains a series of salutations to various aspects of the linga, culminating in a prayer for supreme yoga, progeny, and eternal dharma.

beats.title: Royal Consecration (Rājābhiṣeka)
beats.summary: Agni instructs Vaśiṣṭha on the royal consecration (rājābhiṣeka) ritual, detailing the duties of a king, selection of a priest, ministers, and queen, purification with various clays, anointing with five substances by different castes, and the mantras and procedures for the ceremony.

beats.title: Chapter 219
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana contains the mantras for the royal consecration (abhiṣeka) of a king. It invokes a vast array of deities, sages, celestial beings, and natural elements to bless and protect the king, ensuring his victory and prosperity.

beats.title: Sahāyasampatti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Sahāyasampatti' (Acquisition of Allies), provides a comprehensive guide for a king on selecting and managing ministers, officials, and servants. It covers the qualities of various functionaries such as the commander-in-chief, gatekeeper, envoy, treasurer, physician, elephant keeper, and fort superintendent. It also discusses the conduct of servants towards the king, the characteristics of a good fort, and duties of a king towards his subjects, including protection of women and Brahmins. The chapter emphasizes the importance of loyalty, discretion, and proper behavior for those in royal service.

beats.title: Rajadharma: Duties of a King
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the duties and responsibilities of a king (rajadharma), including the hierarchy of local administrators, protection of subjects, taxation, handling of lost property, and punishment for crimes. It emphasizes the king's role as a protector and the consequences of neglecting this duty.

beats.title: King's Duties: Inner Palace Management and Cosmetics
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 223) discusses the duties of a king regarding the management of the inner palace (antaḥpura), particularly the behavior and signs of affection or disaffection in women, and provides recipes for perfumes, mouth fresheners, and other cosmetics. It emphasizes the need for kings to protect women but not trust them completely.

beats.title: Rājadharma: Duties of a King
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 224) focuses on the duties of a king (rājadharma), including the education and protection of princes, the importance of self-control, the avoidance of vices, the conquest of enemies through a systematic approach, the use of spies, the conduct of war, and the welfare of subjects. It also emphasizes the role of divine and human effort in governance.

beats.title: Rājadharmāḥ: The Duties of a King
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rājadharmāḥ' (Duties of a King), discusses the balance between fate (daiva) and human effort (pauruṣa), emphasizing the superiority of personal endeavor. It outlines seven diplomatic upāyas (means): sāma (conciliation), upapradāna (gifts), bheda (dissension), daṇḍa (punishment), māyā (deception), upekṣā (neglect), and indrajāla (illusion). The king is compared to various deities and natural forces, highlighting his role as protector and punisher.

beats.title: Rajadharmah: The Duties of Kings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rajadharmah' (Duties of Kings), is a discourse by Pushkara on the administration of justice. It details various fines and punishments for crimes such as theft, false witness, breach of contract, damage to property, assault, adultery, and misuse of royal decrees. The text prescribes specific fines in gold coins (karshapana, suvarna) and corporal punishments like mutilation or death for severe offenses, with variations based on caste (varna).

beats.title: Yuddhayātrā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yuddhayātrā' (War Expedition), provides guidance for a king on when and how to embark on a military campaign. It discusses favorable omens, seasons for different types of armies, and bodily twitches as auspicious or inauspicious signs.

beats.title: Svapnādhyāya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Svapnādhyāya' (Chapter on Dreams), enumerates various dream symbols and their auspicious or inauspicious meanings. It also prescribes remedial rites for bad dreams, such as bathing, worship of deities, and recitation of hymns. The timing of dream fruition is explained based on the watch of the night.

beats.title: Omens and Auspicious Signs
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 229) discusses omens (śakunāni) and auspicious/inauspicious signs, including dreams, sounds, and sights, particularly for a king. It lists various items and events that are considered auspicious or inauspicious, and prescribes remedies like worshiping Hari (Viṣṇu) to remove inauspiciousness.

beats.title: Omens (Śakuna) and Their Interpretation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses omens (śakuna) and their interpretation for predicting auspicious and inauspicious outcomes. It classifies omens based on direction, time, sound, and behavior of animals and birds, providing guidelines for divination in various contexts such as travel, battle, and daily life.

beats.title: Śakunāni: The Science of Omens
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Śakunāni' (Omens), provides a detailed exposition on interpreting various animal omens, particularly crows, dogs, jackals, cows, horses, elephants, and others, for predicting outcomes in warfare, travel, and daily life. It describes auspicious and inauspicious signs based on the behavior, direction, and sounds of these animals, offering guidance for kings and warriors.

beats.title: Omens and Statecraft for a King's Expedition
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses auspicious and inauspicious omens for military expeditions (yātrā), including planetary positions, lunar days, directions, and animal behaviors. It also outlines the seven limbs of a kingdom (saptāṅga) and the concept of maṇḍala (circle of states) for a king's strategic planning.

beats.title: The Sixfold Policy and Deceptive Tactics
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'ṣāḍguṇya', continues the discourse on statecraft and military strategy. Puṣkara explains the two types of daṇḍa (punitive action) — open and covert — and discusses the sixfold policy (sandhi, vigraha, yāna, āsana, dvaidhībhāva, saṃśraya). It also describes māyopāya (deceptive tactics) such as creating false omens, using fire, and displaying severed heads to demoralize the enemy.

beats.title: The Daily Routine of a King
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the daily routine (prātyahikarājakarma) for a king, including waking up before dawn, listening to music and spies, attending to bodily functions, bathing, performing sandhyā and japa, worshipping Vāsudeva, offering oblations, giving cows to Brahmins, looking in a mirror, consulting physicians, receiving blessings from the guru, holding court, hearing cases, consulting ministers in secret, exercising, inspecting troops and weapons, eating, studying scriptures, and retiring for the night.

beats.title: Raṇadīkṣā: The War Initiation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Raṇadīkṣā' (War Initiation), provides a detailed ritual and strategic guide for a king preparing for war. It covers the seven-day preparation period including worship of deities, dream interpretation, formation of battle arrays (vyūhas), deployment of troops, and ethical conduct during war. The chapter emphasizes the importance of divine propitiation, strategic planning, and moral rules of engagement.

beats.title: The Shri Stotra: Hymn to Goddess Lakshmi for Royal Stability
beats.summary: Indra praises Goddess Shri (Lakshmi) as the source of all prosperity and victory, and requests her to never abandon him. Pushkara then advises that reciting this Shri-stotra grants worldly enjoyment and liberation.

beats.title: Rama's Teachings on Statecraft
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 237 presents the political and ethical teachings (nīti) spoken by Rama to Lakshmana, originally taught by Pushkara. It covers the fourfold duties of a king, the importance of self-control, the six internal enemies, the four sciences, and the conduct of a virtuous ruler. The chapter emphasizes dharma, compassion, and wise governance.

beats.title: Rājadharmāḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rājadharmāḥ' (Duties of Kings), presents a discourse by Rama on the seven limbs of a kingdom (svāmya, amātya, rāṣṭra, durga, koṣa, bala, suhṛt), the qualities of a king, ministers, priests, and subjects, as well as the characteristics of a good country, fort, treasury, army, and friend. It also outlines the conduct of servants and the means of acquiring a friend.

beats.title: Ṣāḍguṇya: The Sixfold Policy of Kings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Ṣāḍguṇya' (The Sixfold Policy), is a discourse by Rama on statecraft and military strategy. It details the mandala theory of twelve kings, the sixfold policy (sandhi, vigraha, yāna, āsana, dvaidha, saṃśraya), types of alliances and wars, causes of conflict, and conditions for peace or war. The teaching emphasizes pragmatic decision-making based on strength, time, and circumstances.

beats.title: Ṣāḍguṇya: The Sixfold Policy of Statecraft
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Ṣāḍguṇya' (the sixfold policy), presents a discourse by Rama on statecraft, diplomacy, and military strategy. It covers the five limbs of counsel (mantra), the qualities of envoys, types of spies, the sixfold royal policy (sandhi, vigraha, yāna, āsana, dvaidha, saṃśraya), the three powers (prabhāva, utsāha, mantra), the seven constituents of a state (svāmi, amātya, janapada, durga, kośa, daṇḍa, mitra), and the methods of sāma, dāna, bheda, daṇḍa, and māyā.

beats.title: Rājanīti: The Science of Statecraft and Military Formation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rājanīti' (Statecraft), provides a comprehensive guide on military strategy, including the six types of forces, formation (vyūha) arrangements, battle tactics, and the treatment of soldiers and commanders. It emphasizes the importance of proper formation, deception, and psychological warfare, and concludes with a reference to Rāma's teachings used by Lakṣmaṇa to defeat Indrajit.

beats.title: Puruṣalakṣaṇa: Characteristics of Men
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 242 describes the auspicious and inauspicious bodily characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of men and women, as narrated by Samudra to Garga. It lists various types of men based on physical traits, such as ekādhika, dviśukra, trigambhīra, etc., and explains their qualities.

beats.title: Strīlakṣaṇa: Characteristics of Women
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the auspicious and inauspicious physical characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of women, as taught by Samudra. It lists ideal features such as a beautiful body, elephant-like gait, full hips, blue-black hair, and a pleasing demeanor, while warning against undesirable traits like quarrelsome nature or hairy body.

beats.title: The Origin of the Sword Nandaka and Auspicious Marks of Royal Emblems
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the characteristics of a virtuous woman (strīlakṣaṇa) and then focuses on the auspicious marks and construction of royal emblems such as the chowrie (cāmara), umbrella (chatra), throne (bhadrāsana), bow (dhanus), and sword (khaḍga). It includes a mythological account of the origin of the sword Nandaka, given by Brahmā to Viṣṇu, and prescribes rituals for their worship.

beats.title: Ratnaparīkṣā (Examination of Gems)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the characteristics and classifications of various precious gems (ratnas) for kings to wear. It lists types of gems, their desirable qualities (such as internal luster, purity, and proper shape), and warns against flawed or dull stones. The chapter also includes brief notes on swords from the previous chapter.

beats.title: Vāstulakṣaṇa: Characteristics of a House
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Vāstulakṣaṇa', describes the characteristics of land and the construction of a house according to Vastu principles. It details the types of soil, the layout of the Vastu Purusha mandala with deities in various positions, and rituals for laying bricks and planting trees.

beats.title: Puṣpādipūjāphalaṃ (The Fruit of Worship with Flowers, etc.)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the benefits of worshipping Vishnu with various flowers and leaves, and the effects of different types of water and substances on trees. It also mentions the use of ghee, milk, and other items for bathing Vishnu and the resulting rewards.

beats.title: Dhanurveda: The Science of Archery and Warfare
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 248) is a treatise on dhanurveda (the science of archery and warfare). It classifies weapons, combat techniques, stances, and the roles of different varnas in warfare, emphasizing the importance of proper training and posture.

beats.title: Archery Instructions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed instructions on archery, including the preparation of the bow and arrow, proper stance, aiming techniques, and various types of targets. It is a technical manual for archery practice.

beats.title: The Science of Archery and Weaponry
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 250) focuses on the art of archery and weaponry, detailing techniques for mastering the bow, arrow, noose, sword, and armor. It emphasizes the importance of a steady hand, focused mind, and disciplined practice to achieve success in combat.

beats.title: Dhanurveda Kathana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Dhanurveda Kathana', is a technical manual on the science of weapons and warfare. It enumerates and classifies various techniques for sword and shield, noose, spear, mace, club, and other weapons, as well as formations and combat methods. The speaker is Agni, who imparts this knowledge to a listener (likely a sage or warrior).

beats.title: Vyavahāra: The Science of Legal Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the principles of legal procedure (vyavahāra), including its classification into four feet, eight limbs, eighteen titles, and various subcategories. It outlines the roles of the king, judges, witnesses, and the process of litigation, emphasizing justice and the avoidance of bias.

beats.title: Vyavahāra (Legal Procedure) - Debt and Surety
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 253) deals with legal procedures related to debt, repayment, and sureties. It outlines rules for debt recovery, interest rates, responsibilities of heirs, and conditions under which debts must be repaid. The text also covers the roles of sureties and the treatment of pledges.

beats.title: Divine Ordeals and Witnesses
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the qualifications of witnesses (sākṣi) in legal proceedings, the consequences of false testimony, the proper documentation of debts and pledges, and the procedures for divine ordeals (divya) such as fire, water, and poison tests to determine truth.

beats.title: Dāyavibhāgakathanam (Laws of Partition and Inheritance)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the laws of inheritance and partition of property (dāyavibhāga). It covers various scenarios including division by the father, rights of wives and daughters, shares of sons of different varṇas, types of sons (aurasa, kṣetraja, etc.), and rules for inheritance in absence of direct heirs.

beats.title: Determination of Boundary Disputes and Other Legal Matters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses legal procedures for resolving boundary disputes, land ownership, animal trespass, property recovery, interest rates, slavery, and duties of guilds and assemblies. It provides rules for determining boundaries using witnesses and landmarks, penalties for false claims, and regulations for grazing, cultivation, and trade.

beats.title: Legal Penalties for Offenses
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 257) deals with legal punishments for various offenses including verbal abuse, physical assault, property damage, fraud, and theft. It prescribes fines and corporal penalties based on the severity of the crime and the social status of the victim.

beats.title: Ṛgvidhāna: The Ritual Use of Ṛgvedic Mantras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Ṛgvidhāna', provides a detailed guide on the ritual use of Ṛgvedic mantras for various worldly and spiritual benefits. It is presented as a dialogue between Agni and Puṣkara, covering topics such as japa (chanting), homa (offerings), and specific mantras for health, wealth, victory, and liberation. The chapter emphasizes the power of the Gāyatrī mantra and the syllable Oṃ, and includes instructions for using mantras in agriculture, warfare, and daily life.

beats.title: Yajurvidhana: The Science of Yajurveda Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Yajurvidhana', provides a comprehensive guide to the ritual use of Yajurveda mantras for various worldly and spiritual goals. It details specific homa (fire offerings) with different materials like ghee, grains, and herbs to achieve desires such as wealth, health, victory, and liberation. The text also includes mantras for protection, healing, and overcoming enemies, emphasizing the power of precise ritual action.

beats.title: Sāmavidhāna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Sāmavidhāna', provides instructions on the use of Sāma Veda mantras for various purposes such as purification, protection, healing, prosperity, and victory in battle. It details specific mantras and rituals for different outcomes, including removing sins, curing diseases, gaining wealth, and ensuring success in war.

beats.title: Atharvavidhana: The Rituals of the Atharvaveda
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Atharvavidhana', describes the rituals and mantras of the Atharvaveda for various purposes such as peace, health, prosperity, and protection. It lists specific ganas (groups of mantras) and their effects, along with offerings and materials used in homa (fire sacrifice).

beats.title: Utpātaśānti: Pacification of Portents
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 262) deals with the pacification of portents (utpātaśānti). It describes the Śrīsūkta and its variants in different Vedas, the Puruṣasūkta, and various śāntis (pacification rites) such as Amṛtā, Abhayā, and Saumyā. It also discusses the classification of portents into divine, atmospheric, and terrestrial, and prescribes specific rituals to counteract them, including homa, japa, and worship of deities like Varuṇa, Prajāpati, Śiva, and Parjanya.

beats.title: Devapūjā and Vaiśvadevabali for Pacification of Omens
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the rituals of worship of Vishnu (devapūjā) and the offering of bali (vaiśvadevabali) as a means to counteract evil omens (utpātaśānti). It details the procedure for bathing, offering arghya, pādya, ācamana, and other items, followed by homa (fire sacrifice) with specific mantras. The chapter also includes instructions for offering bali to various deities and beings, including the ancestors (pitṛs), crows, dogs, and cows, and concludes with a list of mantras for expiation of sins.

beats.title: Ritual Bathing for Various Desires
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of bathing (snāna) for various purposes, including worship of Viṣṇu and the directional deities (dikpālas). It details the procedure for preparing the bath, the materials to be used, and the accompanying rites such as drawing a maṇḍala and offering homa.

beats.title: Vināyaka Snāna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual bath (snāna) for appeasing Vināyaka (Gaṇeśa), who causes obstacles. It details the symptoms of Vināyaka's affliction, the procedure for the bath using various herbs, muds, and mantras, and the offerings to be made. The chapter concludes with the benefits of performing this ritual.

beats.title: Māheśvara Snāna and Associated Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Māheśvara Snāna (bath of Śiva) and related rituals, including the use of various substances for bathing, mantras, and homa (offerings). It is narrated by Puṣkara and is said to bring victory and prosperity to kings. The chapter also lists different types of baths and their benefits.

beats.title: Nīrājana Vidhi (Ritual of Lustration)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of Nīrājana (lustration/ waving of lights) for the king's welfare, including the worship of Indra's flag, the installation of a flagstaff, the worship of Bhadrakālī, and the procedure for the Nīrājana ceremony involving elephants, horses, and the king's regalia.

beats.title: Mantras for Royal Insignia
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Chatrādimantrādayaḥ', provides mantras and rituals for consecrating royal insignia such as umbrella, horse, banner, sword, armor, and drum. It includes invocations to various deities and cosmic forces to ensure victory, protection, and prosperity for the king. The chapter also describes the divine origins and powers of these objects, and prescribes their worship and anointment.

beats.title: Viṣṇu Pañjara
beats.summary: Puṣkara narrates the Viṣṇu Pañjara (Viṣṇu's cage) mantra, a protective shield described by Brahmā to Śaṅkara (Śiva) before the destruction of Tripura. The mantra invokes Viṣṇu's forms and weapons (discus, mace, bow, sword) placed in cardinal and intermediate directions to guard against all evil beings.

beats.title: The Glorification of the Vedas and the Agni Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 270) discusses the classification and enumeration of the Vedas, their branches (shakhas), and the role of Vyasa in dividing them. It also describes the transmission of the Puranas through Suta Lomaharshana and his disciples, and extols the Agni Purana itself as a manifestation of Vishnu, promising various boons to those who read or hear it.

beats.title: Merits of Donating Puranas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the merits of donating various Puranas on specific lunar days, along with appropriate gifts like cows (dhenu). It lists the names, verse counts, and associated deities or sages for each Purana, and concludes with instructions for honoring the reciter and performing rituals at the completion of the Mahabharata.

beats.title: Suryavamsa Kirtana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana narrates the solar dynasty (Suryavamsa) lineage, starting from Brahma and Marichi, through Kashyapa, Vivasvan, and Manu Vaivasvata, listing kings like Ikshvaku, Mandhata, Harishchandra, Sagara, Bhagiratha, Dilipa, Raghu, Aja, Dasharatha, and Rama, along with their descendants.

beats.title: The Lunar Dynasty (Somavaṃśa)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the lunar dynasty (Somavaṃśa), starting from Brahma's creation of Atri, then Soma (the Moon), his abduction of Tara (wife of Brihaspati), leading to the Tārakāmaya war, and the birth of Budha. It continues with the lineage of Pururavas, his relationship with Urvashi, and subsequent kings including Ayu, Nahusha, and Raji, who aided the gods in battle and later had his sons tricked by Brihaspati.

beats.title: Yadu Dynasty and the Syamantaka Jewel
beats.summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana describes the Yadu dynasty lineage, starting from Yadu's sons and continuing through various kings, including Kārtavīrya Arjuna, and culminating in the ancestors of Krishna, such as Vasudeva and Devakī. It also recounts the story of the Syamantaka jewel and Krishna's role in retrieving it.

beats.title: The Twelve Great Battles and the Avatars of Viṣṇu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the twelve great battles (dvādaśasaṅgrāmāḥ) fought between the devas and asuras, and the avatars of Viṣṇu who protected the devas. It also details the lineage of Kṛṣṇa, his wives, sons, and the Yādava clan, emphasizing Kṛṣṇa's role as the protector of dharma.

beats.title: Genealogy of Kings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 276) provides a genealogical list of kings from the Puru and Yadu dynasties, tracing lineages from Turvasu, Druhyu, and others down to various kings and regions.

beats.title: Chapter 277
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 277) provides a detailed genealogy of the Puru dynasty, tracing the lineage from Puru through various kings and sages, including the Kauravas and Pandavas, up to Janamejaya. It lists numerous rulers, their descendants, and notable figures such as Bharata, Dhanvantari, and Kṛpa.

beats.title: Siddhauṣadhāni: The Science of Ayurveda
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a comprehensive treatise on Ayurveda, attributed to Dhanvantari, covering treatments for various diseases including fever, diarrhea, tumors, skin disorders, respiratory ailments, eye diseases, and poison. It details dietary regimens, herbal remedies, and therapeutic procedures such as emesis, purgation, and enema.

beats.title: Sarvarogaharāṇyauṣadhāni (Remedies for All Diseases)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification of diseases (śārīra, āgantuka, sahaja), their causes, and treatments. It describes the three doshas (vāta, pitta, kapha), their locations in the body, seasons of aggravation and pacification, and the properties of tastes (rasas) and potencies (vīrya). It also includes rituals for healing, such as offerings to planets and recitation of hymns.

beats.title: Rasadilakshana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rasadilakshana', presents the teachings of Dhanvantari on the properties of tastes (rasa), potencies (virya), post-digestive effects (vipaka), and the preparation of medicines. It classifies tastes into sweet, sour, salty (somaja) and pungent, bitter, astringent (agneya), and discusses their effects on doshas and dhatus. The chapter also describes decoction (kashaya), oil-based preparations (snehapaka), lehya, and five types of medicinal preparations (rasa, kalka, shruta, shita, phanta). It emphasizes the importance of diet, sleep, and sexual activity for health, and provides guidelines for massage and seasonal regimens.

beats.title: Vṛkṣāyurveda: The Science of Plant Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled Vṛkṣāyurveda (the science of plant life), is narrated by Dhanvantari. It provides instructions on planting trees, auspicious directions and constellations for planting, watering schedules, soil preparation, and remedies for tree health. The chapter emphasizes the sacredness of trees and their connection to deities.

beats.title: Nānārogaharāṇyauṣadhāni
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Nānārogaharāṇyauṣadhāni' (Remedies for Various Diseases), is a discourse by Dhanvantari on medicinal treatments for a wide range of ailments, including pediatric issues, skin diseases, digestive disorders, and more. It also includes a brief section on alchemical and magical practices.

beats.title: Mantras as Supreme Medicine
beats.summary: Dhanvantari teaches that mantras are the supreme medicine. He lists various mantras (Omkara, Gayatri, Om Namo Narayanaya, etc.) and names of Vishnu that grant health, wealth, liberation, and victory. The chapter emphasizes that dharma is the great medicine and that reciting these mantras cures diseases and fulfills desires.

beats.title: Mṛtasañjīvanīkarasiddhayoga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Mṛtasañjīvanīkarasiddhayoga', presents a dialogue between Dhanvantari and Ātreya on medicinal formulations (siddhayogas) for various ailments. It includes mantras for health and longevity, and details numerous herbal remedies for fevers, cough, skin diseases, eye disorders, and more, emphasizing the use of specific plants and preparations.

beats.title: Kalpasāgara: The Ocean of Alchemical Elixirs
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Kalpasāgara', presents a dialogue where Dhanvantari teaches various alchemical and herbal preparations (siddhayogas) that grant longevity, cure diseases, and conquer death. The formulations involve ingredients like triphalā, bilva, nirguṇḍī, and others, often combined with honey, ghee, or milk, and taken over periods ranging from months to years to achieve health, youth, and extended lifespan.

beats.title: Gajacikitsā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Gajacikitsā' (Elephant Treatment), provides a comprehensive guide on elephant care, including characteristics of good elephants, diseases, and treatments. It covers topics such as elephant anatomy, auspicious signs, dietary recommendations, medicinal preparations, and therapeutic procedures for various ailments. The chapter is narrated by sage Pālakāpya to King Lomapāda.

beats.title: Aśvavāhanasāra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Aśvavāhanasāra' (Essence of Horse Riding), is narrated by Dhanvantari. It covers the science of horse riding and veterinary care, including auspicious times for acquiring horses, proper riding techniques, training methods, and the symbolic placement of deities on the horse's body. It also describes horse breeds, their characteristics, and a mantra for consecrating the horse.

beats.title: Aśvacikitsā: The Science of Horse Care
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 288) deals with the characteristics and treatment of horses (aśvacikitsā). It describes auspicious and inauspicious physical features, various ailments and their remedies, including herbal formulations, dietary regimens, and seasonal care.

beats.title: Aśvaśānti (Horse Pacification)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Aśvaśānti' (Horse Pacification), is narrated by Śālihotra. It describes rituals for pacifying horses, including daily, occasional, and desire-motivated rites. The procedures involve worship of deities like Śrīdhara, Uccaiḥśravas, Viṣṇu, Śrī, Brahmā, Śaṅkara, Soma, Āditya, Aśvins, Revanta, and Dikpālas, along with offerings, oblations, and donations to Brahmins, aimed at promoting horse health and prosperity.

beats.title: Gajaśānti: The Pacification of Elephants
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of Gajaśānti, a ceremony for pacifying elephants. It details the worship of various deities, including Viṣṇu, Lakṣmī, Brahmā, Śiva, and others, along with the elephant Airāvata and other mythical elephants. The procedure involves constructing a maṇḍala, offering oblations, and reciting mantras to ensure the elephant's well-being and protection for the king.

beats.title: The Sanctity of Cows and Rituals for Their Protection
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, spoken by Dhanvantari, focuses on the sanctity and protective power of cows. It prescribes rituals for cow worship, including the use of cow products (milk, ghee, curd, urine, dung) for purification and atonement. It details various penances (krcchra) involving cow products, describes the benefits of cow service, and outlines a ceremony for cow protection (gośānti) involving offerings to deities and the worship of cows for prosperity and removal of afflictions.

beats.title: Mantraparibhāṣā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Mantraparibhāṣā', provides a detailed classification and methodology for mantras. It discusses types of mantras (male, female, neuter), their characteristics (āgneya, saumya), rules for initiation, and the importance of proper recitation. It also lists various deities associated with tithis and vāras, and describes the nyāsa of letters on the body.

beats.title: Characteristics of Snakes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the characteristics (lakṣaṇa) of snakes (nāgas), including their types, anatomy, behavior, and the auspicious or inauspicious signs associated with snake bites. It also covers the classification of snakes by caste, the effects of their venom, and the interpretation of omens related to snakes and messengers.

beats.title: Treatment of Snakebite and Poisoning
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 294) provides detailed instructions for treating snakebite and other poisonings using mantras, meditation, and herbal remedies. It describes the classification of poisons (animal and plant), the use of specific mantras dedicated to deities like Nīlakaṇṭha (Śiva) and Garuḍa, and elaborate visualization and nyāsa (placement of mantras on the body) techniques. The chapter emphasizes the power of mantra and meditation to neutralize poison, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga framework of inner purification and energy control.

beats.title: The Five-Limbed Rudra Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the five-limbed (pañcāṅga) Rudra ritual, detailing the mantras, ṛṣis, chandas, and deities associated with each limb. It emphasizes the efficacy of this ritual for destroying poison and diseases, and includes specific mantras for protection.

beats.title: Viṣahṛn Mantra and Oṣadhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides mantras and herbal remedies for curing snakebite and other poisons. It includes invocations to Rudra and various deities, along with practical instructions for preparing antidotes using plants like śirīṣa, marica, and others.

beats.title: Treatments for Venom and Poison
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana provides detailed medical treatments for various types of snake bites, insect stings, and other venomous conditions. It includes mantras, herbal remedies, and dietary prescriptions for neutralizing poison. The chapter also describes a ritual for collecting herbs and a protective mantra.

beats.title: Balagrahaharabalatantram
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Balagrahaharabalatantram', is a tantric manual for treating infants afflicted by various female demonic entities (grahis). It describes symptoms for each day, month, and year of affliction, along with prescribed rituals including bali (offerings), dhupa (incense), lepa (ointments), and mantra recitation to pacify the grahis and protect the child.

beats.title: Mantras and Rituals to Counteract Malevolent Planetary Spirits
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes mantras and rituals to counteract the effects of malevolent planetary spirits (grahas) that cause various forms of madness and afflictions. It details the origins of these grahas from Rudra's anger, their characteristics, and the methods to propitiate them through offerings, meditation, and mantra recitation.

beats.title: Surya Worship and Ganesha Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 300) focuses on the worship of the Sun (Surya) and includes mantras, rituals, and offerings. It also contains a brief section on a mantra for Ganesha and describes the procedure for Surya worship with specific offerings and postures.

beats.title: Various Mantras and Rituals for Worldly Goals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 301) presents various mantras and rituals for achieving specific worldly goals such as subjugation (vaśīkaraṇa), attraction, and obtaining sons. It includes instructions for worship of deities like Sarasvatī, Śakra (Indra), and Cāmuṇḍā, along with herbal recipes and practices for personal gain.

beats.title: Ritual of Nyasa and Worship of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of nyasa (placement of mantras on the body) and worship of Vishnu, including the eight-syllable mantra, the arrangement of deities and powers on the lotus of the heart, and the offering of upacaras.

beats.title: Rituals of the Five-Syllable Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the rituals and mantras associated with the five-syllable (pañcākṣara) mantra of Shiva, including initiation (dīkṣā), nyāsa, and worship procedures. It details the symbolic correspondences of the mantra's syllables to elements, senses, and deities, and outlines the steps for purifying the disciple and performing the ritual.

beats.title: The Fifty-Five Names of Vishnu and Their Sacred Places
beats.summary: This chapter lists 55 names of Vishnu associated with specific sacred places (tīrthas), describing the benefits of reciting them. It also includes instructions for ritual practices such as homa and abhiṣeka.

beats.title: Mantras and Rituals of Narasimha and Others
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents mantras and rituals associated with Narasimha and other deities, focusing on subjugating enemies, curing diseases, and removing obstacles. It includes detailed instructions for homa (fire offerings), yantra drawing, and the use of specific materials like ghee, sesame, and wood. The chapter emphasizes the power of the Narasimha mantra for protection and destruction of evil forces.

beats.title: The Trailokyamohana Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Trailokyamohana mantra, a powerful mantra for attracting and controlling all beings in the three worlds. It includes the mantra itself, instructions for nyasa, meditation, worship, and homa (fire sacrifice) to achieve various desires such as subjugation, attraction, and fulfillment of wishes. The deity is Vishnu in a captivating form, accompanied by Lakshmi and other goddesses.

beats.title: Worship of Trailokyamohana and Durgā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of the goddess Trailokyamohana (Lakshmi) and the goddess Durgā, including mantras, rituals, yantras, and meditations for attaining wealth, power, and victory. It details the iconography of the goddesses and their attendants, as well as the benefits of performing specific homas and japa.

beats.title: Worship of Tvarita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of the goddess Tvarita, including her mantra, nyasa, meditation on her form, and the method of japa and puja. It also mentions that Shiva and Parvati once assumed the forms of a hunter and huntress, respectively.

beats.title: The Tvarita Mantra and Ritual
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the Tvarita mantra and its associated deity, a form of the goddess with eighteen arms, seated on a lion. It details the ritual worship, including the drawing of a yantra, the nyasa of mantras, and the use of various offerings for specific results. The text also explains the phonetic construction of the mantra and the mudras to be used.

beats.title: Tvaritāmūlamantrādiḥ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Tvaritāmūlamantrādiḥ', describes the ritual of initiation (dīkṣā) involving the construction of a yantra (sacred diagram) with a lotus and vajra, the placement of mantras, and the performance of homa (fire sacrifice). It details the steps for drawing the yantra, installing deities, and the transformative effects of the ritual, culminating in the disciple's initiation and attainment of various states from stationary beings to ultimate liberation.

beats.title: Tvarita Vidya: The Swift Knowledge of Mantra Siddhi
beats.summary: Agni reveals the Tvarita Vidya, a system of mantra siddhi based on a 9-cell grid (navakoṣṭha) and prastāva (permutation) analysis. The chapter describes methods for drawing yantras, using materials like skulls and shroud cloth, and performing rites for attraction, immobilization, destruction, and pacification, often in cremation grounds.

beats.title: Nānāmantrāḥ: Various Mantras and Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 312) presents a collection of mantras and rituals for worshiping various deities, including Vinayaka (Ganesha), Tripura, Gauri, and others. It details the construction of yantras, meditation practices, and the benefits of chanting specific mantras for protection, victory, and fulfillment of desires.

beats.title: The Worship of Tvarita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship and mantra of the goddess Tvarita, including her iconography, the construction of a yantra for subjugation (nigraha) and grace (anugraha), and the use of various materials and rituals.

beats.title: Mantras for Stambhana and Other Magical Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various mantras and rituals for stambhana (stilling), mohana (delusion), vaśya (control), vidveṣa (enmity), uccāṭana (uprooting), māraṇa (killing), and other magical effects. It includes specific instructions for drawing diagrams, using materials like bhūrja bark, and reciting mantras to achieve these aims.

beats.title: Various Mantras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents various mantras and their applications, including a vidya (knowledge) for all purposes, a mantra for snakebite and enemy destruction, and the Kubjika vidya. The mantras are attributed to Lord Agni and are described as effective for overcoming poison, enemies, diseases, and obstacles, as well as for subjugation and victory.

beats.title: Sakalādimantroddhāra: The Extraction of Mantras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Sakalādimantroddhāra', is a technical exposition on the extraction and construction of mantras, particularly the Prāsāda mantra and its variations (sakala and niṣkala). It describes the components of mantras, including bīja syllables, deities associated with each part, and the process of combining them. The chapter also outlines the five-faced (pañcānana) form of Sadāśiva and the mantra's application for various purposes.

beats.title: Gaṇapūjā Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of Gaṇapūjā (worship of Gaṇapati), including the construction of a maṇḍala, the invocation of various forms of Gaṇapati, and the recitation of mantras such as the Aghorāstra and a Gāyatrī dedicated to Śiva. It provides detailed instructions for the geometric layout, colors, and deities to be worshipped.

beats.title: Worship of Vagishvari
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of Vagishvari (Sarasvati), including the construction of a mandala, the mantra, the deity's iconography, and the procedure for japa and initiation.

beats.title: Description of Mandalas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various mandalas (sacred diagrams) used in worship, focusing on the construction and symbolism of lotus-shaped designs. It details the layout, colors, and deities associated with different parts of the mandala, emphasizing their use for achieving worldly desires and liberation.

beats.title: Astraśānti and Grahaśānti Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ritual of astrayāga (weapon worship) and various śānti (pacification) rites to counteract negative omens, planetary afflictions, and disturbances caused by spirits. It prescribes specific homas (offerings) using substances like ghee, til (sesame), and guggulu to neutralize various calamities.

beats.title: Pāśupataśānti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents the Pāśupataśānti, a ritual for pacification using the Pāśupata weapon. It contains a long mantra invoking Lord Śiva in his fierce form as Mahāpāśupata, listing his attributes and various astra (weapon) mantras ending with 'phaṭ'. The chapter states that reciting the mantra once destroys all obstacles, one hundred times pacifies calamities and ensures victory in battle, and offerings of ghee and guggulu can accomplish even impossible tasks.

beats.title: Mantras and Rituals for Protection and Power
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various mantras and rituals for protection, subjugation, and attainment of desires. It includes the ṣaḍaṅga (six-limbed) mantras, the mantra for Durgā, the Mahāmṛtyuñjaya mantra, and the five-faced Rudra mantras (Pañcabrahma). The text provides instructions for japa, homa, and the use of herbal mixtures for purposes such as curing diseases, attracting women, and defeating enemies.

beats.title: Rudraśānti: The Pacification through Aghora
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Rudraśānti', describes the worship and mantra of Aghora, a fierce form of Shiva, for pacification and fulfillment of desires. It details the colors, rituals, and benefits of the Aghora mantra, including protection from evils, diseases, and enemies, and outlines the construction of a yantra with various deities and tattvas.

beats.title: Classification of Rudraksha and Mantras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification and characteristics of Rudraksha beads, the types of mantras based on their syllables and effects, the association of different parts of the body with elements, and the methods for using mantras for various purposes such as peace, attraction, and destruction. It also describes the different types of disciples based on their spiritual lineage and the effects of mantras based on their initial and final syllables.

beats.title: The Worship of Gauri
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the worship of Gauri (Uma), including mantras, meditation, mandala, mudras, homa, and the benefits of the ritual. It details the iconography of Gauri, the associated deities, and the procedures for puja and offerings.

beats.title: Glory of Temples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the glory of temples (devālayamāhātmya), including the worship of Śiva liṅga, the use of rudrākṣa beads, the significance of the five-syllable and six-syllable mantras, and various rituals for purification and prosperity.

beats.title: Temple Construction and Prosody
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the construction and worship of temples and lingas, emphasizing that the merit gained is multiplied by the material used. It also introduces the science of prosody (chandas) as taught by Pingala, detailing the classification of syllables and metrical feet.

beats.title: Chandaḥsāra
beats.summary: This chapter, titled 'Chandaḥsāra' (Essence of Metrics), is a discourse by Agni on the rules of Sanskrit prosody. It covers the classification of syllables as light (laghu) or heavy (guru), the definition of metrical feet (gaṇa), and the enumeration of the eight Vasus and other deities associated with meters.

beats.title: Classification of Vedic Meters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification and structure of various Vedic meters (chandas), focusing on the Gayatri meter and its variations, including the number of syllables, feet (padas), and associated deities. It describes how different meters like Ushnik, Anushtup, Brihati, Jagati, and others are formed by modifying the Gayatri pattern, and lists the presiding deities for each meter.

beats.title: Exposition on Meters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification of Vedic meters (chandas) and their sub-types, including gāyatrī, triṣṭubh, jagatī, and others. It describes the structure of meters in terms of syllables, feet (pāda), and gaṇas, and lists various meters such as āryā, vaitālīya, and their variants. The chapter is a technical exposition on prosody.

beats.title: Discussion on Meters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification and characteristics of poetic meters (chandas) and their types: sama (even), ardhasama (half-even), and vishama (uneven). It provides rules for constructing these meters, including variations like pathya, vipula, and capala.

beats.title: Description of Ardhasama Meters
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes various meters (chandas) used in poetry, specifically focusing on the classification and characteristics of ardhasama (half-even) meters. It lists names and structural patterns of different meters such as Upacitraka, Drutamadhyā, Vegavatī, Rudravistāra, etc., detailing their syllabic arrangements and variations.

beats.title: Samavṛttanirūpaṇa (Description of Meters)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 333) is a technical treatise on Sanskrit prosody, specifically describing various poetic meters (vṛttas) such as samavṛtta, vidyunmālā, māṇavakā, etc. It lists the syllabic patterns (gaṇas) and rules for each meter, often naming them after deities or concepts. The chapter is attributed to Agni as the speaker and includes references to traditional authorities like Ṛṣis.

beats.title: Prastāranirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Prastāranirūpaṇa' (Exposition of Prosody), provides a technical exposition on metrics (chandas) and the method of prastāra (arrangement of syllables). It describes rules for determining the structure of verses, including the use of guru (heavy) and laghu (light) syllables, and the meruprastāra method for calculating combinations.

beats.title: Śikṣānirūpaṇam
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 335) is titled 'Śikṣānirūpaṇam' (Exposition of Phonetics). It describes the science of pronunciation and phonetics, including the classification of vowels, consonants, their places of articulation, and the proper method of utterance for achieving spiritual merit.

beats.title: Characteristics of Poetry and Drama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the characteristics and classification of poetry (kāvya) and drama (nāṭaka). It covers definitions of dhvani, varṇa, pada, vākya, and the three types of kāvya: prose (gadya), verse (padya), and mixed (miśra). It elaborates on sub-types of prose, the structure of mahākāvya, and various poetic forms like kalāpa, kulaka, muktaka, and koṣa. The chapter emphasizes the importance of rasa (aesthetic emotion) as the life of poetry.

beats.title: Enumeration of Dramatic Forms
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana enumerates and defines the 27 types of dramatic performance (rūpaka), including nāṭaka, prakaraṇa, ḍima, etc. It discusses the general and specific characteristics of drama, the importance of rasa, the structure of the pūrvaraṅga (preliminaries), and the components such as nāndī, sūtradhāra, and the invocation of deities and gurus.

beats.title: Śṛṅgārādirasanirūpaṇam
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the theory of rasa (aesthetic emotion) in drama and poetry. It begins by describing the ultimate reality as the supreme Brahman, from which arises the sense of ego (ahaṅkāra), then abhimāna (self-importance), and then rati (love), which when fully developed becomes śṛṅgāra (erotic rasa). It enumerates the eight primary rasas: śṛṅgāra, hāsya, karuṇa, raudra, vīra, bhayānaka, vībhatsa, adbhuta, and śānta. It explains how rasas arise from the interplay of sattva, rajas, and tamas, and from specific emotions (sthāyibhāvas) like rati, hāsa, śoka, etc. It also describes the thirty-three vyabhicāribhāvas (transitory emotions), the four types of nāyakas (heroes), and the roles of pīṭhamarda, viṭa, and vidūṣaka as companions. The chapter emphasizes that a poet creates a world of rasa, and without rasa, poetry is lifeless.

beats.title: Rīti-nirūpaṇa (Description of Styles)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the concept of 'rīti' (style) in literary composition, describing four types: Pāñcālī, Gauḍī, Vaidarbhī, and Lāṭajā. It also elaborates on the four 'vṛttis' (dramatic styles): Bhāratī, Ārabhaṭī, Kauśikī, and Sātvatī, with details on their characteristics and components.

beats.title: Description of Body Movements in Dance and Drama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 340) describes the various movements and gestures (angakarma) used in dance and drama, categorizing them by body parts such as head, eyebrows, eyes, nose, lips, chin, neck, hands, chest, abdomen, sides, shanks, and feet. It enumerates specific types of movements and their classifications, providing a technical manual for performance arts.

beats.title: Abhinaya and Rasa Theory
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the theory of abhinaya (dramatic representation) and the classification of rasas (aesthetic sentiments). It describes the four types of abhinaya: sattvika (mental), vacika (verbal), angika (physical), and aharya (costume). It then elaborates on the srngara (erotic) rasa, its two divisions (sambhoga and vipralambha), and further subdivisions. The chapter also covers hasya (comic), karuna (pathetic), raudra (furious), and vira (heroic) rasas, detailing their causes and manifestations.

beats.title: Śabdālaṅkāras: Verbal Figures of Speech
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses śabdālaṅkāras (verbal figures of speech), including anuprāsa (alliteration), yamaka (paronomasia), citra (pictorial poetry), and various types of bandha (patterns). It provides definitions, classifications, and examples of these poetic devices.

beats.title: Arthālaṅkāra Nirūpaṇa
beats.summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana discusses arthālaṅkāras (figures of speech based on meaning). It begins by defining arthālaṅkāra as the ornamentation of meaning, without which even beautiful words are not charming. It then enumerates eight primary types: svarūpa (natural description), sādṛśya (similarity), utprekṣā (poetic fancy), atiśaya (hyperbole), vibhāvanā (causal impossibility), virodha (contradiction), hetu (cause), and sama (equality). Under sādṛśya, it lists four subtypes: upamā (simile), rūpaka (metaphor), mahokti (exaggeration), and arthāntaranyāsa (generalization). The chapter then elaborates on upamā (simile), describing its various forms based on the presence or absence of compounds, the expression of common quality, and the relationship between upamāna and upameya. It details eighteen types of upamā, including dharmavastūpame, parasparopamā, viparītopamā, niyamopamā, aniyamopamā, samuccayopamā, vyatirekopamā, bahūpamā, and mālopamā.

beats.title: Alaṅkāraśāstra in the Agni Purāṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the alaṅkāras (ornaments) of poetry, focusing on śabdālaṅkāra (sound figures) and arthālaṅkāra (sense figures). It defines six aspects of poetic expression: praśasti (praise), kānti (beauty), aucitya (propriety), saṃkṣepa (brevity), yāvadarthatā (adequacy), and abhivyakti (manifestation). It further elaborates on types of śruti (hearing) and the distinction between mukhya (primary) and upacārika (secondary) meanings.

beats.title: Discussion on the Qualities of Poetry
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the qualities (guṇas) of poetry, distinguishing between general and specific qualities, and elaborates on the seven qualities of sound (śabda-guṇas): śleṣa (compactness), lālitya (grace), gāmbhīrya (depth), saukumārya (delicacy), udāratā (nobility), ojas (vigor), and prasāda (clarity). It also touches on the relationship between qualities and figures of speech, and the importance of context in determining whether a feature is a quality or a defect.

beats.title: Kāvyadoṣaviveka (Analysis of Poetic Faults)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the classification of poetic faults (doṣas) in kāvya. It enumerates seven types of faults related to the speaker (vaktṛ), the word (vācaka), and the meaning (vācya), and further subdivides them into categories such as asādhutva, aprayuktatva, chāndasatva, avispaṣṭatva, kaṣṭatva, asāmayikatva, and grāmyatva. It also covers common faults like kriyābhraṃśa, visandhi, punarukta, and vyastasambandhatā, and discusses exceptions where certain faults are acceptable in difficult compositions.

beats.title: Ekākṣarābhidhāna: The One-Syllable Lexicon
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 347) is titled 'Ekākṣarābhidhāna' (The One-Syllable Lexicon). It provides a detailed esoteric mapping of Sanskrit phonemes (from 'a' to 'kṣa') to deities, concepts, and cosmic principles. It then prescribes mantras and rituals for worshiping various forms of Durgā, Gaṇeśa, and other deities, emphasizing the power of single-syllable mantras for liberation and worldly benefits.

beats.title: Exposition of Grammar (Vyākaraṇa)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a concise exposition of Sanskrit grammar (vyākaraṇa), focusing on the pratyāhāra system (abbreviated phonetic groupings) used in Pāṇinian grammar. It lists the pratyāhāra sūtras and explains how they function to derive grammatical rules, aimed at instructing students like Kātyāyana and beginners.

beats.title: Rules of Sandhi in Sanskrit Grammar
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the rules of sandhi (euphonic combination) in Sanskrit grammar, specifically vowel sandhi and consonant sandhi, with examples. It also mentions the pratyāhāra (abbreviated grammatical formulas) and provides various illustrative compounds.

beats.title: Vibhakti Siddharūpa (Declension of Nouns)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 350) is a grammatical treatise on the declension of nouns (subanta) in Sanskrit, following the system of Kātyāyana. It lists various noun stems (prātipadika) and their inflected forms across cases and numbers, focusing on masculine, feminine, and neuter genders. The chapter is a technical manual for correct word formation, with no narrative or mythological content.

beats.title: Feminine Gender Noun Declensions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana presents a grammatical exposition of feminine gender noun forms (strīliṅgaśabdasiddharūpa) in Sanskrit, listing declensions of various feminine nouns such as ramā, jarā, sarvā, buddhī, nadī, kumārī, śrī, strī, dhenu, jambū, varṣābhū, punarbhū, mātṛ, gau, nau, vāc, sraj, vidvas, bhavatī, dīvyantī, bhātī, tudantī, etc.

beats.title: Napumsakaśabdasiddharūpaṃ
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana, titled 'Napumsakaśabdasiddharūpaṃ', is a grammatical treatise on neuter gender words. It lists various neuter nouns and their forms, likely as part of a larger section on Sanskrit grammar. The chapter is narrated by Skanda.

beats.title: Grammatical Exposition on Neuter Gender and Kāraka
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses grammatical rules for neuter gender words (napuṃsakaśabda) and then introduces the topic of kāraka (case relations). It provides examples of various grammatical forms and explains the different types of kartā (agent), karma (object), and karaṇa (instrument).

beats.title: Discourse on Compound Words
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the grammatical concept of samāsa (compound words), classifying them into various types such as nitya, anitya, tatpuruṣa, karmadhāraya, bahuvrīhi, etc., with examples.

beats.title: Taddhita Suffixes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana discusses the grammatical topic of taddhita (secondary suffixes) in Sanskrit. It lists various suffixes and their meanings, with examples such as 'vatsala', 'picchila', 'dantura', 'madhura', 'keśara', 'rajasvala', 'dhanī', 'hastī', 'payasvī', 'māyāvī', 'vācāla', 'phalin', 'varhiṇ', 'kekī', 'vṛndāraka', 'śītālu', 'himālu', 'vātula', 'vāśiṣṭha', 'kaurava', 'pāñcāla', 'māthura', 'cāndraka', 'kramaka', 'kauśaka', 'praiyaṅgavīnaka', 'maudgīna', 'kaudravīṇa', 'vaideha', 'dākṣi', 'dāśarathi', 'nārāyaṇādika', 'āśvāyana', 'gārgya', 'vātsaka', 'vainateya', 'cāṭakera', 'gaudheraka', 'gaudhāra', 'kṣatriya', 'kulīna', 'kairavya', 'sugandhi', 'nabhastārakita', and others. The chapter is a technical exposition of Sanskrit grammar.

beats.title: Uṇādi Suffixes and Their Meanings
beats.summary: This chapter of the Agni Purana (Chapter 356) is a grammatical treatise on the uṇādi suffixes (uṇādayaḥ pratyayāḥ) that are added to verbal roots (dhātu) to form nouns. It lists various words derived through these suffixes, along with their meanings, such as 'uṇi' meaning a craftsman, 'gomāyu' as a jackal, 'kṛkavāku' as a cock, etc. The chapter is technical and focuses on linguistic derivation rather than narrative.

beats.title: Exposition of Verb Inflections
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 357) is a technical exposition on Sanskrit grammar, specifically the tiṅ (verb) inflections. It covers the three voices (active, passive, impersonal), tenses and moods (present, imperative, optative, imperfect, aorist, perfect, periphrastic future, simple future, conditional), and the personal endings for the two conjugations (parasmaipada and ātmanepada). It also lists a number of verbal roots (dhātus) with their meanings.

beats.title: Grammatical Forms of √bhū
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana is a grammatical treatise on Sanskrit verb forms (tiṅanta), specifically focusing on the root √bhū (to be) and its derivatives, including kṛt suffixes, tense formations, and nominal derivatives. It is a technical text with no narrative or mythological content.

beats.title: Chapter 359
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana is a lexicon of synonyms and classifications for various categories of existence, including celestial beings, natural phenomena, time divisions, virtues, sounds, musical instruments, and dramatic arts. It serves as a reference for names and attributes of deities, demons, and other entities, as well as terms related to cosmology, ethics, and aesthetics.

beats.title: Avyaya-varga: The Section on Indeclinables
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 360) is a grammatical treatise listing indeclinable words (avyayas) and their meanings, organized by semantic categories. It provides definitions and usage contexts for particles like ā, ku, ca, svasti, svit, sakṛt, etc., as well as interjections and other indeclinables.

beats.title: Nānārthavargāḥ (Chapter on Homonyms)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists homonyms and synonyms for various words, covering a wide range of meanings from celestial realms to everyday objects, emotions, and actions. It is a lexical compilation without narrative or teaching.

beats.title: Nānārthavarga: Bhūmi, Vana, Oṣadhi, Siṃha, etc.
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 362) is a lexicon listing synonyms and names for various categories: earth, cities, buildings, trees, plants, herbs, animals, and birds. It is a continuation of the nānārthavarga (section on synonyms) and provides a comprehensive vocabulary for these subjects.

beats.title: Synonyms for Human Categories and Body Parts
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists various Sanskrit synonyms for human social categories (varnas), family relations, life stages, diseases, and body parts. It is a lexical compilation without narrative.

beats.title: Definitions of Ritual and Spiritual Terms
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 364) defines terms related to lineage, ritual roles, and spiritual practices. It covers concepts such as family lineage (vaṃśa, gotra), types of priests (ṛtvij, adhvaryu, udgātṛ, hotā), sacrificial implements (caṣāla, yūpa), offerings (āmikṣā, pṛṣadājya, pāyasa), and ascetic types (bhikṣu, parivrāṭ, yati). The chapter also discusses synonyms for worship (pūjā, namasyā, arcā) and the distinction between primary and secondary rituals (mukhya kalpa, anukalpa).

beats.title: Kṣatraviṭśūdravargaḥ — The Classes of Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Shudras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana defines the terminology and hierarchy of the Kshatriya (warrior/ruler), Vaishya (merchant/farmer), and Shudra (servant) classes, including various titles, roles, and objects related to warfare, administration, and daily life. It also covers weights, measures, metals, and professions.

beats.title: Sāmānyanāmaliṅgāni (Common Names and Characteristics)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana lists synonyms and definitions for various qualities, behaviors, and states of beings, categorized by social classes (kshatriya, vaishya, shudra). It provides a lexicon of terms describing virtues, vices, and characteristics.

beats.title: The Four Types of Dissolution
beats.summary: This chapter describes the four types of pralaya (dissolution): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛta (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It focuses on the naimittika pralaya at the end of a kalpa, where a severe drought occurs for a hundred years, Vishnu absorbs all waters, seven suns appear and burn the three worlds, then Rudra (from Vishnu's breath) burns everything, and beings move to higher lokas.

beats.title: Ātyantika Laya and the Cycle of Rebirth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the ultimate dissolution (ātyantika laya) and the process of rebirth. It details the types of suffering (ādhyātmika, ādhibhautika, etc.), the journey of the soul after death through the ātivāhika body, the role of Yama and Citragupta, the stages of embryonic development, and the classification of guṇas (sāttvika, rājasa, tāmasa) as they manifest in human nature.

beats.title: Śarīrāvayavāḥ (The Parts of the Body)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the anatomy and physiology of the human body, including the sense organs, motor organs, the five great elements, the 24 tattvas, the seven or eight āśayas (receptacles), the formation of various organs from the combination of humors (vāta, pitta, kapha) and tissues (rasa, rakta, māṃsa, etc.), and the locations of prāṇa. It also discusses the role of the puruṣa (jīva) and the ultimate goal of knowing the supreme puruṣa.

beats.title: Narakanirūpaṇa: Description of Hells
