beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana describes the process of death, the journey of the soul, and the various hells (narakas) that sinners experience. It details how the vital airs leave the body, the soul's transition through an intermediate body (ātivāhika deha), and the enumeration of 28 crore hells with their names and torments. The chapter concludes by emphasizing that knowledge and yoga, along with austerities, donations, and worship of Vishnu, can destroy the cycle of rebirth.

beats.title: Yamaniyamāḥ
beats.summary: Agni Purana Chapter 371, titled 'Yamaniyamāḥ', presents the eight limbs of yoga (aṣṭāṅgayoga) with a focus on the first two: yama (restraints) and niyama (observances). It details the five yamas—ahiṃsā (non-violence), satya (truthfulness), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacarya (celibacy), and aparigraha (non-possessiveness)—and the five niyamas—śauca (purity), santoṣa (contentment), tapas (austerity), svādhyāya (self-study), and īśvarapūjana (worship of God). The chapter elaborates on ahiṃsā as the highest dharma, defines truthfulness, and extensively discusses brahmacarya, including its eight aspects and the dangers of women and intoxicants.

beats.title: Instructions on Āsana, Prāṇāyāma, and Pratyāhāra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 372) provides detailed instructions on the practices of āsana (posture), prāṇāyāma (breath control), and pratyāhāra (withdrawal of senses) as means to attain mental concentration and spiritual purification. It describes the proper setup for meditation, the technique of prāṇāyāma with its three phases (recaka, pūraka, kumbhaka), the classification of prāṇāyāma by duration, and the benefits of controlling the senses. The chapter also includes analogies such as the body as a chariot and the mind as the charioteer, emphasizing the importance of discipline for self-realization.

beats.title: The Teaching on Meditation (Dhyāna)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana defines meditation (dhyāna) as continuous contemplation of Viṣṇu, describes the fourfold aspects of meditation (meditator, meditation, object, purpose), and outlines the practice of inner worship through meditation as superior to external rituals. It details the visualization of a lotus within the body, the syllable Oṃ, and the form of Viṣṇu, emphasizing that meditation leads to liberation and the eight siddhis.

beats.title: Dhāraṇā: The Practice of Concentration
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 374) explains the practice of dhāraṇā (concentration) as a precursor to dhyāna (meditation) and samādhi (absorption). It describes various types of dhāraṇā—āgneyī (fiery), vāruṇī (watery), aiśānī (supreme), and amṛtātmikā (nectar-like)—and their effects on the body and mind. The text emphasizes fixing the mind on a chosen object (lakṣya) and gradually withdrawing from external distractions, leading to spiritual liberation.

beats.title: The Nature of Samādhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 375) defines samādhi as a state of deep meditation where the mind becomes still like a lamp in a windless place, merging with the divine. It describes the characteristics of a yogi in samādhi, the obstacles and attainments (siddhis) that arise, and the process of liberation through knowledge. The chapter also discusses the subtle anatomy of light rays (raśmis) within the heart, leading to higher realms.

beats.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
beats.summary: Agni reveals the knowledge of Brahman (brahmajñāna) for liberation from the ignorance of samsara. The self (ātman) is not the body, senses, mind, or prāṇa, but the witness and experiencer distinct from them. The creation proceeds from Brahman to ether, air, fire, water, earth, and the subtle and gross bodies. The meditator should contemplate the dissolution of the gross body into Brahman.

beats.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
beats.summary: Agni reveals the nature of the supreme Brahman as the supreme light, negating all attributes and identifications with the gross, subtle, and causal bodies, states of consciousness, and cosmic principles.

beats.title: Brahmajñāna: The Knowledge of Brahman
beats.summary: Agni reveals the five paths to liberation: through sacrifices one attains the realm of Virāj, through austerity a higher realm, through renunciation of actions one merges with Prakṛti, through knowledge one attains Kaivalya. He explains detachment, renunciation, and the nature of knowledge as discrimination between sentient and insentient. The supreme Self, called Viṣṇu, is the support of all. He is worshipped through pravṛtti (active) and nivṛtti (renunciate) paths. The chapter also discusses the two kinds of Brahman: śabdabrahman (scriptural) and parabrahman (transcendental), and the meaning of the syllable 'bhaga' as denoting opulence.

beats.title: The Teaching of Non-Dual Knowledge (Advaita Brahma Jnana)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Agni Purana (Chapter 379) presents the teaching of Advaita Brahma Jnana (non-dual knowledge of Brahman) through two dialogues: first between a king and a brahmana (who is an avadhuta), and second between the sages Rtu and Nidagha. The brahmana, who had previously been a deer in a past life due to attachment, now teaches the king about the illusory nature of the self and the world, culminating in the realization that all is one with Vasudeva (Vishnu). The story of Rtu and Nidagha further illustrates non-duality through a parable about a king and an elephant.

beats.title: Gītāsāra
beats.summary: This chapter, titled 'Gītāsāra' (Essence of the Gita), is a concise summary of the Bhagavad Gita's teachings as narrated by Agni. It covers key philosophical concepts such as the nature of the self (ātman), detachment, karma yoga, jñāna yoga, bhakti yoga, the field and knower of the field (kṣetra-kṣetrajña), the three guṇas, and the path to liberation.

beats.title: Yamagītā
beats.summary: Yama teaches Naciketas the Yamagītā, a discourse on the highest good for humans, citing various teachers (Kapila, Pañcaśikha, Gaṅgāviṣṇu, Janaka, Brahmā, Jaigīṣavya, Devala, Sanaka) and culminating in the path to liberation through knowledge, yoga, and realization of Viṣṇu as the supreme Brahman.

beats.title: Glorification of the Agni Purana
beats.summary: This chapter glorifies the Agni Purana, describing it as the supreme Purana that embodies both para and apara vidya (higher and lower knowledge). It extols the benefits of reading, hearing, writing, and donating the Purana, claiming it removes sins, grants worldly pleasures and liberation, and protects from calamities. The chapter also narrates the origin of the Purana from Agni and its transmission through Vasistha, Vyasa, and Suta to the sages at Naimisharanya.

beats.title: Cosmic Time and Creation
beats.summary: Suta narrates the cosmic time cycles, including the definitions of kāṣṭhā, kalā, muhūrta, day and night for humans and gods, yugas, manvantaras, and the day of Brahmā. It describes creation and dissolution within a kalpa, and mentions the Matsya and Varāha avatāras.

beats.title: Secondary Creation and the Five Sargas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the secondary creation after the first chapter's primary creation. While Vishnu (Narayana) sleeps, a lotus emerges from his navel, from which Brahma is born. Brahma is instructed to create, but when Vishnu disappears, Brahma becomes angry and Rudra (Shiva) is born from his anger. Brahma names him Rudra. Then Brahma creates Daksha and other Prajapatis. The chapter also details the five types of creation (sargas): Mahat, Tanmatras, Vaikarika, Mukhya (immobile beings), Tiryakshrota (animals), and Urdhvasrota (deities). The creation is described as a process of Brahma's contemplation.

beats.title: Creation of the Sages and Beings
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation process, listing the primary sages (Sanaka, Marīci, etc.) born from Brahmā, their roles in pravṛtti and nivṛtti paths, and how Dakṣa's daughters gave rise to all beings.

beats.title: The Secondary Creation and Genealogies
beats.summary: This chapter describes the secondary creation (pratisarga) by Rudra and the sages, focusing on the origin of Rudra's eleven forms, the marriage of Dharma to the daughters of Daksha, and the genealogies of various beings including gods, demons, sages, and others, as narrated by Suta to Bharadvaja.

beats.title: The Birth of Vasishtha and Agastya from Mitra and Varuna
beats.summary: Suta narrates the story of Vasishtha's birth as the son of Mitra and Varuna, explaining how the sage was born from a lotus after Mitra and Varuna's seed fell in three places. The chapter also lists Daksha's daughters given to Kashyapa, the twelve Adityas, and describes Varuna's celestial city Sukhavati. It concludes with the benefits of reciting this narrative.

beats.title: Mārkaṇḍeya Conquers Death
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Mārkaṇḍeya, a sage who conquered death through devotion to Viṣṇu. It begins with Bharadvāja asking Sūta how Mārkaṇḍeya defeated death. Sūta recounts that Mārkaṇḍeya was born to Mṛkaṇḍu and Sumitrā, but a prophecy said he would die at age twelve. His parents were distressed, but Mārkaṇḍeya resolved to conquer death through tapas. He went to the forest, met his grandfather Bhṛgu, who advised him to worship Nārāyaṇa (Viṣṇu). Following this, Mārkaṇḍeya performed severe penance and ultimately conquered death by the grace of Viṣṇu.
